AI.tldr: This paper models multi-agent LLM deliberation as a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden internal belief (anchor) that pulls its opinion alongside neighbor influence. The anchor can be recovered from deliberation traces, explains why agent confidence can exceed initial beliefs (escaping the convex hull), and provides a generalization test to check anchor-driven behavior. Experiments across three model families show anchor influence is a spectrum, not binary.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM deliberation is increasingly used to improve reasoning, but how and why it works is rarely formally modeled. The authors aim to bridge this gap by drawing parallels with opinion-dynamics models from social science, capturing both the group influence (herd effect) and individual internal beliefs (anchors) that classical models like DeGroot and Friedkin-Johnsen cannot fully explain.
AI.method: The authors propose a closed-loop dynamical system where each agent has a hidden internal anchor that continually pulls its opinion regardless of neighbors. They derive conditions under which the anchor is recoverable from deliberation data, show that it enables confidence to escape the convex hull of initial beliefs, and propose a generalization test (predicting held-out runs) to validate whether a model is truly anchor-driven. Experiments are conducted across three open-weight model families to measure anchor influence.
AI.result: The recovered anchor explains behavior forbidden by classical consensus rules: agent confidence can climb past the highest initial belief. The generalization test reveals that anchor-driven behavior is a spectrum across model families, not all-or-nothing. All anchors exert approximately equal influence strength, but differ in where they sit; only when the anchor sits far from initial opinions does deliberation escape the convex hull and require the full closed-loop model.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent deliberation dynamics are better captured by a closed-loop model with hidden anchors than by classical consensus rules. The spectrum of anchor influence suggests different model families exhibit varying degrees of internal-belief-driven behavior. The proposed test (predicting held-out runs) offers a practical check for determining when anchor modeling is necessary.

title: PrefSQA: Pairwise Preference Prediction for Speech Quality Assessment and the Critical Role of High Quality Datasets
summary: Mean opinion scores (MOS) are widely used for speech quality assessment, yet scalar labels are sensitive to rater variability and listening test differences. This introduces labeling noise, which limits the reliability of MOS prediction. Preference prediction reduces this variability as listeners compare signals directly, producing cleaner labels. We study MOS-free preference prediction and propose PrefSQA, which incorporates uncertainty-aware logits, an impairment attention head, and a module based on non-matching-reference comparisons. We use and refine five datasets, including MOS-derived and low-noise simulated sets with matching and non-matching content, experiment with human preference sets, and test on unseen data. Experiments show small improvements on MOS-derived data, while other sets reveal clear improvement over the baselines, highlighting the value of high-quality preference data and demonstrating the effectiveness of the proposed method.

AI.tldr: PrefSQA proposes a MOS-free preference prediction approach for speech quality assessment, using uncertainty-aware logits, impairment attention, and non-matching-reference comparisons. It shows modest gains on MOS-derived data but clear improvements on cleaner preference datasets.
AI.motivation: MOS labels are sensitive to rater variability and listening test differences, introducing labeling noise that limits prediction reliability. Preference prediction reduces this variability by having listeners compare signals directly, producing cleaner labels.
AI.method: PrefSQA incorporates (1) uncertainty-aware logits, (2) an impairment attention head, and (3) a module based on non-matching-reference comparisons. The method uses five datasets including MOS-derived and low-noise simulated sets with matching and non-matching content.
AI.result: Small improvements on MOS-derived data; clear improvement over baselines on low-noise simulated sets (with matching and non-matching content) and human preference sets, especially on unseen data.
AI.conclusion: High-quality preference data yields more reliable speech quality assessment than MOS-derived labels, and the proposed PrefSQA method effectively leverages such data for improved prediction.

title: Interpretable and Verifiable Hardware Generation with LLM-Driven Stepwise Refinement
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in software development. However, they are susceptible to hallucinations, meaning that they can introduce subtle semantic and logical errors. Due to the high stakes in chip design and manufacturing, hardware engineers are still reluctant to rely on LLMs for register-transfer level (RTL) generation. In this paper, we propose a hardware generation framework that combines the creativity and broad knowledge of LLMs with the explainability and mathematical rigor of formal methods. Specifically, we devise a set of transformation rules that cover various design decisions and hardware features. By iteratively applying these rules, an LLM agent can convert a design specification into an RTL program with guaranteed correctness. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness and efficiency of the framework.

AI.tldr: A hardware generation framework combining LLMs with formal methods to produce correct RTL code from specifications using transformation rules.
AI.motivation: LLMs hallucinate subtle semantic/logical errors, making hardware engineers reluctant to use them for RTL generation due to high stakes in chip design and manufacturing.
AI.method: Devises a set of transformation rules covering design decisions and hardware features; an LLM agent iteratively applies these rules to convert a design specification into a formally guaranteed correct RTL program.
AI.result: The framework demonstrates effectiveness and efficiency in generating correct RTL code.
AI.conclusion: Combining LLMs with formal methods (via transformation rules) yields correct-by-construction RTL generation, addressing the reliability gap that limits LLM adoption in hardware design.

title: PerceptionDLM: Parallel Region Perception with Multimodal Diffusion Language Models
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress in visual understanding tasks. However, most existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation, which limits their efficiency for perception tasks that require captioning multiple regions. In this work, we propose PerceptionDLM, a multimodal diffusion language model optimized for efficient parallel region perception. Built upon PerceptionDLM-Base, a strong foundational baseline that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source diffusion MLLMs, our architecture fully leverages the parallel decoding nature of DLMs. Specifically, we introduce efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable simultaneous perception of multiple masked regions, allowing the model to generate region descriptions in parallel at both the sequence and token levels. This design significantly improves inference efficiency compared with existing approaches that process regions sequentially. To systematically evaluate the parallelism property of visual perception capability for DLMs, we construct a new Parallel Detailed Localized Captioning Benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) by scaling the DLC-Bench to include multiple region masks per image, enabling joint evaluation of both caption quality and inference efficiency. Experiments demonstrate that PerceptionDLM maintains competitive performance in region captioning while achieving substantial speed improvements for multi-region perception tasks. Our results highlight the potential of multimodal diffusion language models for efficient, parallel visual perception. To the best of our knowledge, we are the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception by leveraging the advantages of diffusion language models. Code, models, and datasets are released.

AI.tldr: PerceptionDLM is a multimodal diffusion language model that uses parallel decoding to efficiently caption multiple image regions simultaneously, outperforming sequential autoregressive MLLMs in speed while maintaining competitive quality.
AI.motivation: Existing MLLMs rely on autoregressive generation which limits efficiency for perception tasks requiring captioning of multiple regions, as they process regions sequentially.
AI.method: PerceptionDLM leverages a diffusion language model (DLM) backbone (PerceptionDLM-Base) with efficient prompting and structured attention masking to enable parallel region perception at both the sequence and token levels.
AI.result: PerceptionDLM maintains competitive region captioning quality while achieving substantial inference speed improvements for multi-region tasks. A new benchmark (ParaDLC-Bench) is introduced for evaluating parallelism in visual perception.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal diffusion language models show strong potential for efficient parallel visual perception, and this work is the first to achieve parallel region caption and perception using DLMs.

title: Information Lattice Learning as Probabilistic Graphical Model Structure Learning
summary: Information lattice learning (ILL) learns interpretable rules of a signal by alternately projecting the signal onto a partition lattice that encodes a hierarchy of abstractions and lifting selected rules back to the signal domain. When the signal is a probability mass function, we show the probabilistic rules learned by ILL admit a natural probabilistic graphical model (PGM) interpretation and develop this interpretation in detail. A partition in ILL induces a deterministic quotient variable, and a rule is the marginal law of that quotient variable. A rule set is therefore a collection of marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions. General lifting is the feasible family of all joint distributions satisfying those constraints, while special lifting chooses a maximum-ignorance reconstruction, implemented in ILL by an L2 uniformity principle closely related to maximum entropy. Under a Shannon-entropy lifting, the same constraints yield a log-linear factor graph whose factors are indexed by learned abstractions. The information lattice itself, however, is not a Bayesian network: its edges encode refinement and coarsening of abstractions, not conditional dependence. Thus ILL is best viewed as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables. This view clarifies how ILL relates to graphical models and maximum entropy models, while suggesting new directions for inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning.

AI.tldr: Information Lattice Learning (ILL) learns interpretable probabilistic rules by projecting signals onto partition lattices; these rules correspond to marginal constraints over quotient variables, and the paper develops their interpretation as constraint-based factor graphs (log-linear models) under maximum-entropy-like lifting, distinguishing ILL from Bayesian networks.
AI.motivation: To bridge Information Lattice Learning (ILL) with probabilistic graphical models (PGMs) by showing that the rules learned by ILL are marginal constraints over interpretable abstractions (quotient variables), and that lifting corresponds to reconstructing joint distributions from these constraints.
AI.method: The paper formalizes: (1) partitions induce deterministic quotient variables; (2) rules are marginal laws of these quotients; (3) general lifting yields all joint distributions satisfying those marginal constraints; (4) special lifting (L2 uniformity, closely related to maximum entropy) selects a unique reconstruction; (5) under Shannon-entropy lifting, constraints yield a log-linear factor graph indexed by learned abstractions.
AI.result: ILL rules correspond to marginal constraints over quotient variables; general lifting gives the feasible family of joint distributions; special L2-uniformity lifting (max-entropy-like) picks a unique solution; Shannon-entropy lifting yields a log-linear factor graph where factors are indexed by abstractions. ILL is not a Bayesian network—its edges encode refinement/coarsening, not conditional dependence.
AI.conclusion: ILL is best understood as structure learning for interpretable constraint-based factor graphs over quotient variables, offering new perspectives on inference, identifiability, and hybrid symbolic-probabilistic learning, distinct from standard Bayesian network structure learning.

title: DeXposure-Claw: An Agentic System for DeFi Risk Supervision
summary: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly: they over-read weak evidence and recommend high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations offer no regulator-aligned way to measure the resulting false alarms. We introduce DeXposure-Claw, a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model, forecasts future exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios then turn those forecasts into typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; and (3) data-health and confidence gates constrain escalation before DeXposure-Claw emits auditable supervisory tickets with rationales. We further develop DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness, whose decision axis scores tickets against a regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and an explicit false-intervention rate. Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support our system. Code is at https://github.com/EVIEHub/DeXposure-Claw.

AI.tldr: DeXposure-Claw is a forecast-grounded agentic supervision system that uses a graph time-series foundation model (DeXposure-FM) to predict future exposure networks in decentralized finance, combined with deterministic monitors, stress scenarios, data-health/confidence gates, and auditable supervisory tickets. The system is evaluated via DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis harness including a regulator-aligned decision metric and false-intervention rate, on five years of real weekly data.
AI.motivation: Decentralized finance exposes supervisors to fast-moving, networked credit risks. General-purpose LLM agents fit this setting poorly by over-reading weak evidence and recommending high-stakes interventions, while existing evaluations lack regulator-aligned metrics to measure resulting false alarms.
AI.method: DeXposure-Claw routes LLM decisions through structured evidence: (1) DeXposure-FM, a graph time-series foundation model forecasting exposure networks; (2) deterministic monitors and stress scenarios producing typed alerts, attribution signals, and scenario evidence; (3) data-health and confidence gates constraining escalation; (4) DeXposure-Bench, a six-axis evaluation harness with regulator-aligned absolute-loss ground truth and explicit false-intervention rate.
AI.result: Experiments on five years of weekly real data fully support the system's effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: The proposed system addresses the limitations of general-purpose LLM agents in decentralized finance supervision by providing structured, forecast-grounded, and auditable supervisory decisions with regulator-aligned evaluation metrics.

title: RIVET: Robust Idempotent Voice Attribute Editing
summary: Voice attribute editing models modify characteristics such as age and gender while preserving speaker identity. In large-scale speech datasets, however, attribute annotations are often noisy or inconsistent, which can cause conditional generative models to produce unstable edits. In this work, we show that idempotency provides an effective mechanism for improving robustness to noisy labels. An idempotent operator is one for which repeated application does not change the result, i.e., f(f(x)) = f(x). Enforcing this property acts as an implicit regularizer that reduces sensitivity to mislabeled examples. We introduce RIVET, a training framework that incorporates an idempotency objective to improve robustness to label noise. We evaluate RIVET under controlled label noise and on the GLOBE dataset with naturally noisy annotations. RIVET improves editing success and better preserves speaker identity than standard training, showing that idempotency improves robustness in voice editing models.

AI.tldr: RIVET uses idempotency regularization to make voice attribute editing models robust to noisy labels, improving editing success and speaker identity preservation.
AI.motivation: Attribute annotations in large-scale speech datasets are often noisy or inconsistent, causing conditional generative models to produce unstable edits. The authors aim to improve robustness to these noisy labels.
AI.method: The authors introduce RIVET, a training framework that enforces an idempotency objective (f(f(x)) = f(x)) as an implicit regularizer to reduce sensitivity to mislabeled examples during voice attribute editing.
AI.result: RIVET improves editing success and better preserves speaker identity compared to standard training under controlled label noise and on the GLOBE dataset with naturally noisy annotations.
AI.conclusion: Idempotency effectively improves robustness in voice editing models, providing a mechanism for handling noisy attribute annotations.

title: Beyond the GUI Paradigm: Do Mobile Agents Need the Phone Screen?
summary: Recent advances in mobile agents are dominated by the GUI paradigm, in which agents perceive UI information and emit screen interactions. However, mobile platforms also expose a command-line interface (CLI) that provides direct access to device services and data. We argue CLI deserves first-class consideration alongside GUI. We evaluate three coding agents (Claude Code, Terminus-2, mini-swe-agent) across four model APIs on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld without any mobile-specific post-training, comparing against three reproducible GUI baselines (GUI-Owl-1.5-32B, MAI-UI, Qwen3-VL-32B). Claude Code (Opus 4.7) reaches 71.8\% and 51.9\%, outperforming every reproducible GUI baseline (69.3/68.1/57.8\% on AndroidWorld; 43.2/26.3/13.3\% on MobileWorld), while every other CLI configuration remains competitive. To establish the paradigm's ceiling, we provide oracle CLI solutions that reach 88.8\% on AndroidWorld (103/116 tasks CLI-solvable) and 86.3\% on MobileWorld (101/117 tasks CLI-solvable), indicating substantial room for future improvement. To cover everyday user intents beyond the GUI scope, we introduce the \textbf{CLI-Advantage Task Suite}, comprising 45 templates across five categories: bulk operations, multi-condition filtering, aggregation, cross-app workflows, and hidden device state. Every CLI agent outperforms every GUI baseline in all five categories, with substantially fewer steps per task (10.7 vs.\ 18.6). To support future research on mobile CLI agents, we will open-source agent implementations, oracle solutions, the CLI-Advantage suite, and evaluation infrastructure.

AI.tldr: This paper argues that command-line interface (CLI) agents deserve first-class consideration alongside GUI agents for mobile platforms. Evaluating three coding agents (Claude Code, Terminus-2, mini-swe-agent) across four model APIs on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld, Claude Code (Opus 4.7) achieves 71.8% and 51.9%, outperforming all reproducible GUI baselines. Oracle CLI solutions reach 88.8% and 86.3% on the two benchmarks, showing substantial room for improvement. The paper introduces the CLI-Advantage Task Suite (45 templates across 5 categories) where every CLI agent outperforms every GUI baseline with fewer steps (10.7 vs 18.6). All implementations will be open-sourced.
AI.motivation: Mobile agents are dominated by the GUI paradigm, but mobile platforms also expose a CLI that provides direct access to device services and data. The authors argue CLI deserves first-class consideration alongside GUI, as it has been largely overlooked for mobile agent tasks.
AI.method: The authors evaluate three coding agents (Claude Code, Terminus-2, mini-swe-agent) across four model APIs on AndroidWorld and MobileWorld without any mobile-specific post-training. They compare against three reproducible GUI baselines (GUI-Owl-1.5-32B, MAI-UI, Qwen3-VL-32B). They also provide oracle CLI solutions to establish the paradigm's ceiling and introduce the CLI-Advantage Task Suite covering 45 templates across five categories.
AI.result: Claude Code (Opus 4.7) reaches 71.8% and 51.9%, outperforming every reproducible GUI baseline (69.3/68.1/57.8% on AndroidWorld; 43.2/26.3/13.3% on MobileWorld). Oracle CLI solutions reach 88.8% on AndroidWorld (103/116 tasks CLI-solvable) and 86.3% on MobileWorld (101/117 tasks). Every CLI agent outperforms every GUI baseline in all five CLI-Advantage categories, with substantially fewer steps per task (10.7 vs 18.6).
AI.conclusion: CLI agents are highly competitive with and often superior to GUI agents for mobile tasks, especially for tasks involving bulk operations, multi-condition filtering, aggregation, cross-app workflows, and hidden device state. There remains substantial room for improvement toward oracle-level performance. The authors will open-source all components to support future research.

title: Mix-QVLA: Task-Evidence-Aware Mixed-Precision Quantization of Vision-Language-Action Models
summary: We propose Mix-QVLA, a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models. Mix-QVLA anchors each quantized variant to the full-precision action-token reference decision and evaluates whether quantization preserves task-relevant evidence across key VLA functional boundaries. It computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations and compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, capturing changes in both the strength and allocation of decision-supporting evidence. A soft-bottleneck objective aggregates boundary-level degradation into layer-wise sensitivity scores. Mix-QVLA further models sensitivity throughout task execution, capturing phase-dependent shifts in layer importance rather than assuming a fixed sensitivity profile. The resulting evidence- and time-aware scores guide mixed-precision bit allocation under model-size and BitOps budgets. Extensive evaluations on OpenVLA-style policies show that Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off of low-bit VLA deployment. On LIBERO, Mix-QVLA reduces OpenVLA-OFT memory from 15.4 GB to 4.1 GB, retains 96.3 average success compared with 97.1 for the BF16 model, and achieves a 1.52x inference speedup.

AI.tldr: Mix-QVLA is a task-evidence-aware mixed-precision PTQ framework for VLA models that evaluates quantization effects on decision-supporting evidence across functional boundaries, achieves 4.1GB memory (from 15.4GB), 96.3% success rate, and 1.52x speedup on LIBERO.
AI.motivation: Existing PTQ methods for VLA models lack awareness of how quantization degrades task-relevant evidence that supports action-token decisions across key functional boundaries in the VLA pipeline.
AI.method: Mix-QVLA computes normalized gradient-weighted task-evidence maps from boundary activations, compares full-precision and quantized maps using evidence-mass and attribution-distribution distortion, aggregates degradation via soft-bottleneck objective into layer-wise sensitivity scores, and models sensitivity shifts throughout task execution phases for mixed-precision bit allocation under size and BitOps budgets.
AI.result: On LIBERO with OpenVLA-style policies, Mix-QVLA reduces memory from 15.4GB to 4.1GB, retains 96.3% average success rate (vs 97.1% for BF16), and achieves 1.52x inference speedup.
AI.conclusion: Mix-QVLA improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for low-bit VLA deployment through evidence-aware and time-sensitive mixed-precision quantization.

title: Weibull Weight-Scale Parameter Evolution under AdamW Training Dynamics
summary: Building on a two-parameter Weibull framework for diagnosing transformer weight distributions, we study why the Weibull weight-scale parameter $λ$ grows, overshoots, and then relaxes during AdamW training. We derive a leading-order three-force decomposition of the squared weight norm from the AdamW update: an alignment force measuring the correlation between weights and the adaptive update direction, an injection force from adaptive step magnitude, and a decay force from decoupled weight decay. On self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments, alignment dominates the rise phase, contributing 88-94% of the absolute force budget across four random seeds and remaining robust to super-weight removal. Near saturation, alignment and decay approach balance, explaining the transition from weight-scale growth to relaxation. These force dynamics directly govern the squared-norm component underlying $λ(t)$; the remaining RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is measurable and decomposes into bridge and integration components, totaling approximately 5-6% in densely sampled regions. To extend the analysis to real models where optimizer moments are unavailable, we introduce a spline displacement method that recovers the alignment force from sparse checkpoints with approximately 92-94% accuracy, about twice the naive two-point baseline. We further observe that the peak value of $λ(t)$ varies with training-data coherence in our experiments, suggesting a data-dependent component of weight-scale growth that we leave to a controlled follow-up study. Code and data are available at https://github.com/tiexinding/NPM-Weibull-public.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes why the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ grows, overshoots, and relaxes during AdamW training using a three-force decomposition (alignment, injection, decay) of squared weight norm dynamics, validated on Pythia-70M models, and introduces a spline displacement method to recover alignment forces from sparse checkpoints with ~92-94% accuracy.
AI.motivation: To understand the mechanisms driving the dynamics of the Weibull weight-scale parameter λ during AdamW training, which exhibits growth, overshoot, and relaxation phases.
AI.method: Derives a leading-order three-force decomposition (alignment, injection, decay) of squared weight norm from the AdamW update; validates on self-trained Pythia-70M models with ground-truth optimizer moments; introduces a spline displacement method to recover alignment forces from sparse checkpoints when optimizer moments are unavailable.
AI.result: Alignment dominates the rise phase (88-94% absolute force budget), remains robust to super-weight removal; near saturation alignment and decay approach balance; RMS-to-Weibull reconstruction offset is ~5-6% in densely sampled regions; spline displacement method achieves ~92-94% accuracy vs. ~two times the naive two-point baseline.
AI.conclusion: The three-force framework explains λ(t) dynamics; the spline method enables analysis on real models; data coherence affects λ peak value, suggesting future work on data-dependent components.

title: LLM Doesn't Know What It Doesn't Know: Detecting Epistemic Blind Spots via Cross-Model Attribution Divergence on Clinical Tabular Data
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to structured clinical data, yet whether they can recognize the limits of their own knowledge on such tasks remains unexplored. We study this question through the lens of cross-model attribution divergence with the goal of reducing epistemic uncertainty for structured tasks, comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost on a prediction task via attribution divergence analysis. We report four findings. First, LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous, it outputs a near-constant (0.856-0.937) regardless of whether accuracy is 49% or 75.3%, tracking prompt format rather than prediction quality. Second, the LLM exhibits an inverse difficulty effect: accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct, but matches XGBoost (73.8% vs. 73.1%) when it is moderately uncertain. Third, few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence are orthogonal, super-additive interventions: they reduce the Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS) from 1.54 to 0.38 and improve accuracy from 49% to 75.3% without training. Fourth, a cross-model calibrator that determined LLM reliability using attribution divergence signals reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080, replacing uninformative verbalized confidence with patient-specific reliability estimates, without accessing model internals or requiring repeated inference. We frame these findings as a cold start problem for LLMs on structured data and outline a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness.

AI.tldr: LLMs exhibit poor epistemic self-awareness on structured clinical data tasks, but cross-model attribution divergence analysis (comparing LLM vs. XGBoost) enables calibration and accuracy improvement without training.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether LLMs can recognize the limits of their own knowledge when applied to structured clinical data, and to develop methods for reducing epistemic uncertainty in such settings.
AI.method: Cross-model attribution divergence analysis comparing Qwen 2.5 7B and XGBoost; use of Attribution Disagreement Score (ADS); super-additive intervention combining few-shot examples and SHAP-derived feature evidence; cross-model calibrator using attribution divergence signals.
AI.result: (1) LLM verbalized confidence is epistemically vacuous (constant 0.856-0.937 across accuracy 49%-75.3%); (2) LLM exhibits inverse difficulty effect (accuracy drops to 64.8% when XGBoost is 99% correct); (3) few-shot + SHAP evidence super-additively reduces ADS from 1.54 to 0.38 and improves accuracy from 49% to 75.3%; (4) cross-model calibrator reduces expected calibration error from 0.254 to 0.080.
AI.conclusion: The findings frame LLM limitations on structured data as a cold start problem, and cross-model attribution divergence offers a path toward genuine epistemic self-awareness without internal model access or repeated inference.

title: Latency-Configurable Streaming Speech Enhancement via Asymmetric Temporal Padding
comment: 5 pages, 3 figures. Accepted for presentation at Interspeech 2026
summary: Streaming speech enhancement requires balancing algorithmic latency against quality, yet existing approaches largely treat this as a binary causal versus non-causal choice. LaCo-SENet addresses this issue with two mechanisms parameterized by a single training-time hyperparameter. First, asymmetric temporal padding redistributes past and future context in convolutions, enabling systematic latency configuration. Second, dual-buffer streaming combines state buffers for past context with lookahead buffers that supply future context at both the input and feature levels. Selective state updates also prevent future-frame leakage into the streaming state, ensuring training-inference consistency. On VoiceBank+DEMAND, a fixed-budget (1.37M parameters) backbone yields a family of models spanning 12.5-75.0 ms, with PESQ rising from 3.35 to 3.43. At just 12.5 ms (fully causal), a PESQ of 3.35 matches or exceeds the prior causal state-of-the-art (3.27 at 46.5 ms).

AI.tldr: LaCo-SENet introduces asymmetric temporal padding and dual-buffer streaming to enable systematic latency-quality trade-offs in streaming speech enhancement, achieving causal PESQ of 3.35 at 12.5 ms that matches/exceeds prior causal SOTA (3.27 at 46.5 ms).
AI.motivation: Existing streaming speech enhancement treats latency as a binary causal vs. non-causal choice, lacking systematic mechanisms to balance algorithmic latency and quality.
AI.method: Two mechanisms controlled by a single training-time hyperparameter: (1) asymmetric temporal padding to redistribute past/future context in convolutions; (2) dual-buffer streaming combining state buffers (past) with lookahead buffers (future) at input and feature levels, plus selective state updates to prevent future-frame leakage.
AI.result: On VoiceBank+DEMAND, a 1.37M parameter backbone yields models spanning 12.5-75.0 ms latency with PESQ from 3.35 to 3.43. At 12.5 ms (fully causal), PESQ 3.35 exceeds prior causal SOTA (3.27 at 46.5 ms).
AI.conclusion: LaCo-SENet enables flexible, fine-grained latency-quality trade-offs in a single framework, outperforming prior causal methods at much lower latency.

title: Execution-bound advisory automation for agentic AI: a reproducible AIBOM-driven CSAF-VEX framework
summary: A protocol driven framework is presented that binds SBOM and AIBOM artefacts to deterministic environment capture and structured runtime telemetry. Exploitability is computed from declared artefacts, observed activation conditions, and enforced execution policies. CSAF VEX advisories are generated from combined static and runtime evidence, cryptographically signed, and validated through deterministic replay. Evaluation uses approximately 10000 component entries across synthetic Agentic AI workloads 50 to 5000 components, incorporating OSV, GitHub Advisory, KEV, and EPSS datasets.

AI.tldr: A protocol-driven framework links SBOM/AIBOM artifacts with deterministic runtime telemetry to compute exploitability, generate signed CSAF VEX advisories from static and runtime evidence, and validate via deterministic replay. Evaluated on ~10K components across synthetic AI workloads (50-5000 components) using OSV, GitHub Advisory, KEV, and EPSS datasets.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of assessing exploitability of software and AI components by combining static artifact declarations (SBOM/AIBOM) with runtime telemetry and execution policies, enabling evidence-driven vulnerability advisory generation rather than relying solely on static analysis.
AI.method: The framework binds SBOM and AIBOM artifacts to deterministic environment capture and structured runtime telemetry. Exploitability is computed from declared artifacts, observed activation conditions, and enforced execution policies. CSAF VEX advisories are generated from combined static and runtime evidence, cryptographically signed, and validated through deterministic replay.
AI.result: Evaluation uses approximately 10,000 component entries across synthetic Agentic AI workloads ranging from 50 to 5,000 components, incorporating OSV, GitHub Advisory, KEV, and EPSS datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables deterministic, auditable, and cryptographically verifiable vulnerability advisory generation by combining SBOM/AIBOM artifacts with runtime telemetry, demonstrated at scale across synthetic AI workloads.

title: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings for Controllable and Generalizable Perception
summary: Vision foundation models are typically trained as static feature extractors, placing the burden of task adaptation onto large downstream models. We propose an alternative paradigm: instead of solely feeding visual features into language models, we use language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder. Our method, Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE), leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time, removing the need for task-specific retraining. This enables the encoder to focus on contextually relevant aspects of the input, yielding more controllable and generalizable representations. Empirically, LIVE reduces visual hallucinations (+34 points on MMVP), surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on visual question answering, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks -- offering a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence.

AI.tldr: LIVE uses language to dynamically guide vision encoders at inference time, producing task-centric embeddings that reduce hallucinations, improve VQA, and generalize to unseen instructions without task-specific retraining.
AI.motivation: Vision foundation models are typically static feature extractors, requiring large downstream models for task adaptation. The authors propose using language itself to dynamically guide the vision encoder, removing the need for task-specific retraining and enabling more controllable representations.
AI.method: Language-Instructed Vision Embeddings (LIVE) leverages language as high-level guidance to produce task-centric embeddings at inference time. Instead of solely feeding visual features into language models, the vision encoder is dynamically directed by language instructions.
AI.result: LIVE reduces visual hallucinations by +34 points on MMVP, surpasses vision-language models with orders of magnitude more parameters on VQA, and generalizes to unseen instructions and tasks.
AI.conclusion: LIVE offers a direct path toward adaptive, instruction-driven visual intelligence by using language to guide vision encoders dynamically, eliminating the need for task-specific retraining and achieving strong generalization.

title: Zero-Inflated Gaussian Distributions Enable Parameter-Space Sparsity in Estimation-of-Distribution Algorithms
summary: Estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) are a powerful class of evolutionary methods for black-box optimization, especially when little is known about the structure of the objective. Whereas classical evolutionary algorithms rely on hand-designed mutation and crossover operators, hard to devise for unknown problem structures, and a source of bias, EDAs sidestep operator design entirely: they fit a probability distribution to the best individuals and sample the next generation from it. EDAs are well established on continuous parameter spaces, but they have not previously been generalized to sparse ones, in which most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero. Existing sparse black-box optimizers therefore reintroduce exactly what EDAs were designed to avoid: hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes alternating between support set and active values, zeroing thresholds, and other baked-in assumptions. We close this gap by proposing multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws. A latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions represents sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and the interactions between the two, so sparsity patterns and active values are optimized jointly, hierarchy-free. We show that the latent parameters of this model are identifiable from observed samples, unlike in the missing-data settings where related constructions originate, and introduce practical amortized inversion-based estimators for them. The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures, and on the Lunar Lander benchmark the resulting ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as sampling laws for estimation-of-distribution algorithms (EDAs) to handle sparse black-box optimization, where most optimal solution coefficients are zero. This eliminates the need for hand-crafted sparsity operators by jointly optimizing sparsity patterns and active values via a latent Gaussian model, with practical amortized inversion-based estimators.
AI.motivation: EDAs are powerful for black-box optimization but have not been generalized to sparse parameter spaces (where most coefficients of a good solution are exactly zero). Existing sparse optimizers rely on hand-crafted sparsity operators, bi-level schemes, and zeroing thresholds—precisely what EDAs were designed to avoid.
AI.method: The authors propose multivariate zero-inflated Gaussian (ZIG) distributions as EDA sampling laws, using a latent Gaussian model with separate indicator and value dimensions to represent sparsity patterns, correlations among active parameters, and their interactions. They introduce amortized inversion-based estimators that recover latent correlation structures from observed samples.
AI.result: The estimators accurately recover latent correlation structures. On the Lunar Lander benchmark, the ZIG-EDA converges faster and reaches higher final returns than a dense Gaussian EDA, a hand-crafted sparse evolutionary algorithm, and an ad-hoc sparse EDA, while finding controllers with only a small fraction of parameters active.
AI.conclusion: ZIG distributions enable EDAs to handle sparse black-box optimization without hand-crafted operators, jointly optimizing sparsity and active values in a hierarchy-free manner, with strong empirical results on a control benchmark.

title: REVEAL++: Differentiable Phenotypic Grouping for Vision-Language Retinal Modeling of Alzheimer's Disease Risk
summary: The retina offers a noninvasive window into neurodegenerative disease, capturing subtle structural patterns associated with a risk of future cognitive decline. Vision-language alignment frameworks such as REVEAL have shown that pairing retinal fundus images with structured clinical risk narratives improves early prediction of Alzheimer's disease (AD). A key design choice in these approaches is the use of phenotypic grouping, where individuals with similar risk profiles are treated as multi-positive pairs during contrastive learning. However, existing methods operationalize phenotypic similarity as a discrete construct, relying on hard group assignments that impose rigid supervision and decouple group formation from representation learning. We propose a continuous formulation of phenotypic structure within contrastive learning. Rather than assigning samples to fixed clusters, we model inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function derived from intra-modality embedding similarities in both retinal images and risk profiles. These weights define soft multi-positive relationships through a continuous aggregation operator, enabling graded supervision that reflects the spectrum nature of disease risk. We further introduce a soft-target contrastive objective that jointly learns cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure in an end-to-end manner. Evaluated on UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines. By treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule, our approach provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a continuous formulation of phenotypic similarity for contrastive learning in vision-language alignment (retinal fundus images + clinical risk narratives), replacing discrete group assignments with differentiable weighting functions that enable graded supervision. The method outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning on UK Biobank data for incident AD prediction.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for vision-language alignment in Alzheimer's disease prediction use discrete phenotypic grouping (hard group assignments) that imposes rigid supervision and decouples group formation from representation learning, failing to capture the continuous spectrum of disease risk.
AI.method: Models inter-subject similarity as a differentiable weighting function from intra-modality embedding similarities in retinal images and risk profiles. Uses soft multi-positive relationships via a continuous aggregation operator and a soft-target contrastive objective for end-to-end learning of cross-modal alignment and phenotypic structure.
AI.result: On UK Biobank retinal imaging data for incident AD prediction, the proposed framework consistently outperforms discrete group-based contrastive learning and standard vision-language baselines.
AI.conclusion: Treating phenotypic similarity as a learnable, continuous signal rather than a fixed grouping rule provides a principled and robust foundation for population-scale neurodegenerative risk modeling from multi-modal retinal and clinical data.

title: Exploring Pre-training Benefits on Phoneme Addition through Fine-tuning in Speech Synthesis
summary: Transfer learning is widely used for low-resource text-to-speech. When the target corpus contains phonemes unseen in pre-training, the model must expand its phoneme inventory during fine-tuning; we call the process "phoneme addition." However, it remains unclear whether the pre-trained ability to generate seen phonemes contributes to this process. This study investigates phoneme addition in two settings: (1) a simulation setup using LLM-generated phoneme-controlled corpora that enables investigation without considering confounding factors, and (2) a real-speech cross-lingual transfer setup (English to Japanese) to validate whether the findings hold in practice. Experiments in both settings showed that while fine-tuning achieved higher naturalness than training from scratch, it required as much or more data to achieve comparable PER for new phonemes. These results indicate that pre-training mainly contributes to naturalness improvement, but offers limited benefit for phoneme addition.

AI.tldr: Pre-training helps improve naturalness in low-resource TTS but offers limited benefit for learning new phonemes during fine-tuning, requiring as much or more data for comparable phoneme error rates as training from scratch.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether pre-trained abilities to generate seen phonemes contribute to learning unseen phonemes (phoneme addition) during fine-tuning in low-resource text-to-speech.
AI.method: Two settings: (1) simulation with LLM-generated phoneme-controlled corpora to control confounding factors, and (2) real-speech cross-lingual transfer (English→Japanese) for validation. Compared fine-tuning vs. training from scratch on naturalness and PER.
AI.result: Fine-tuning achieved higher naturalness than training from scratch, but required as much or more data to achieve comparable PER for new phonemes.
AI.conclusion: Pre-training mainly contributes to naturalness improvement but offers limited benefit for phoneme addition.

title: DevOps and General Developers: Insights from Stack Overflow's 2023 Survey
comment: 17 pages, 11 tables, research paper based on the 2023 Stack Overflow Developer Survey data analysis
summary:
Purpose: To investigate the distinct roles of DevOps specialists and general software developers, examining their varying use of tools, technologies, methodologies, and demographics in the current software development environment. In addition, to differentiate these two professional groups regarding their unique contributions and challenges in the field.
  Design/Methodology/Approach: The research uses a quantitative approach to analyze data from the Stack Overflow 2023 Developer Survey. It focuses on a comparative analysis of technological preferences, demographic information, and professional experiences between DevOps specialists and general developers, highlighting key trends and differences. The data analysis was conducted using Python's Pandas library for data analysis.
  Findings: The research indicates no significant difference in the tool and technology preferences between DevOps specialists and general software developers, highlighting their complementary roles. DevOps specialists and general software developers use tools like Docker and Kubernetes, emphasizing efficiency and automation. While general developers employ diverse tools for various role demands, demographic trends show younger general developers and mid-career DevOps professionals. This age range reflects growing experience in DevOps, and both groups are adapting to remote and hybrid work models in the evolving tech industry.
  Practical Implications: This research offers perspectives on the dynamic roles within software development, emphasizing the growing importance of DevOps. It is a valuable resource for academic and industry professionals to understand the evolving dynamics in software development roles.
  Originality/Value: This research fills a significant gap in the existing literature regarding the evolving dynamics of software development roles.

AI.tldr: This study analyzes the Stack Overflow 2023 Developer Survey to compare DevOps specialists and general software developers, finding no significant differences in tool/technology preferences (both use Docker and Kubernetes), but noting demographic distinctions (younger general developers vs. mid-career DevOps professionals) and shared adaptation to remote/hybrid work models.
AI.motivation: To investigate the distinct roles of DevOps specialists versus general software developers, examining their varying use of tools, technologies, methodologies, and demographics, and to differentiate their unique contributions and challenges.
AI.method: Quantitative approach analyzing Stack Overflow 2023 Developer Survey data, using Python's Pandas library for comparative analysis of technological preferences, demographics, and professional experiences.
AI.result: No significant difference in tool/technology preferences; both groups use Docker and Kubernetes. General developers employ diverse tools; demographic trends show younger general developers and mid-career DevOps professionals. Both groups adapting to remote/hybrid work.
AI.conclusion: The roles of DevOps specialists and general developers are complementary rather than distinct in tooling. The research fills a gap in literature on evolving software development role dynamics and offers perspectives for academic and industry professionals.

title: Human-like autonomy emerges from self-play and a pinch of human data
summary: Self-play reinforcement learning has recently emerged as a way to train driving policies without any human data. It uses cheap, large-scale simulations to substitute expensive, large-scale human driving demonstrations. A key limitation of this approach is that policies trained through pure self-play can learn effective but alien driving conventions incompatible with people. Previous works attempt to mitigate such behavioral misalignments through extensive reward engineering and domain randomization, which are brittle and labor-intensive. Instead of completely discarding human demonstrations, our method treats them as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward. Like the spice in a good stew, we find that a little human data goes a long way: our method uses only 30 minutes of human demonstrations, 2500x fewer than comparable imitation learning approaches. Resulting policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories and complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU. Videos and full source code are available at https://spiced-self-play.com/.

AI.tldr: Self-play reinforcement learning for driving policies is enhanced by incorporating a small amount of human demonstration data (30 minutes) as a regularization objective, achieving human-compatible behavior with 2500x less human data than imitation learning, training in 15 hours on a single GPU.
AI.motivation: Pure self-play RL for driving policies produces alien driving conventions incompatible with humans, while prior mitigation through reward engineering and domain randomization is brittle and labor-intensive. The authors aim to achieve human-compatible driving without relying on massive human datasets.
AI.method: Treats human demonstrations as a regularization objective on top of a minimal safe goal-reaching reward in self-play RL. Only 30 minutes of human data is used, significantly less than comparable approaches.
AI.result: Policies coordinate with held-out human trajectories, complete training in 15 hours on a single consumer-grade GPU, and use 2500x less human data than comparable imitation learning methods.
AI.conclusion: A small amount of human data (30 minutes) can effectively regularize self-play RL to produce human-compatible driving policies, avoiding the need for extensive reward engineering or large human datasets.

title: Emergent Alignment
summary: Can Large Language Models (LLMs) discern when their own outputs are misaligned with human ethics? And can they self-correct? We endow an LLM with a conscience step that reviews its own reasoning and outputs, and we extend the training loss with an alignment component using Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to steer the model away from non-ethical outputs. The result is an online technique to align models in a wide range of applications: training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning. It does not require a weaker or stronger judge, relying instead on a frozen copy of itself. In previous work, the Emergent Misalignment scenario showed a range of emergent unethical behaviors from fine-tuning the model to hack code. Instead, we empirically show how to achieve Emergent Alignment: a single high-level introspective question steers training toward an ethical model under the same code hacking scenario.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a method for LLMs to self-detect and self-correct ethical misalignments using a conscience step and DPO-based training, demonstrating emergent alignment in code hacking scenarios without external judges.
AI.motivation: To address the problem of LLMs producing unethical outputs (e.g., Emergent Misalignment from fine-tuning) by enabling models to autonomously recognize and correct their own ethical violations without relying on external judge models.
AI.method: The approach (1) introduces a "conscience step" where the LLM reviews its own reasoning and outputs, (2) extends the training loss with an alignment component via Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to steer away from non-ethical outputs, (3) uses a frozen copy of the model itself (no external judge), and (4) applies a single high-level introspective question to guide training.
AI.result: Empirically demonstrates "Emergent Alignment" — achieving ethical model behavior under the same code hacking scenario that previously caused Emergent Misalignment, using only the proposed self-supervision technique.
AI.conclusion: The proposed online alignment technique is effective across training, fine-tuning, adversarial prompting, and zero-shot learning, enabling LLMs to self-correct ethical misalignment without external judges.

title: PolSeT: Polish Semantics of Timbre Dataset
comment: 8 pages, 7 figures. Data descriptor for the PolSeT dataset (Polish Semantics of Timbre), available at https://doi.org/10.5281/zenodo.17830609 under CC BY 4.0
summary: This data report introduces PolSeT (Polish Semantic Timbre), a dataset designed to facilitate research in psychoacoustics and Music Information Retrieval (MIR) in Polish and cross-cultural contexts. The dataset contains data from two sequential experiments. Experiment 1 (N=60) was a free-verbalization task aimed at creating a lexicon of Polish semantic descriptors. Using 11 stimuli, a total of 1901 descriptors (701 unique) were gathered. Experiment 2 (N=105) utilized this lexicon to conduct a semantic differential study, where participants rated 18 instrument sounds on 8 bipolar scales, with repeated trials for reliability analysis. The released dataset includes raw listener responses, comprehensive demographics (experience, gender, age), audio stimuli, and extracted acoustic features with Python extraction code. This dataset addresses a gap in open timbre research data, providing both the qualitative linguistic groundwork and the quantitative ratings necessary for psychoacoustic research and the training of multilingual semantic embedding models.

AI.tldr: PolSeT is a Polish semantic timbre dataset with 60 participants' free-verbalization data (1,901 descriptors) and 105 participants' semantic differential ratings (18 instrument sounds on 8 bipolar scales), including raw responses, demographics, audio stimuli, and acoustic features.
AI.motivation: To address the gap in open timbre research data, particularly for Polish and cross-cultural contexts, by providing both qualitative linguistic foundations and quantitative psychoacoustic ratings.
AI.method: Two sequential experiments: (1) free-verbalization task (N=60, 11 stimuli) to build a Polish semantic descriptor lexicon; (2) semantic differential study (N=105, 18 instrument sounds, 8 bipolar scales) with repeated trials for reliability analysis.
AI.result: Experiment 1 yielded 1,901 descriptors (701 unique); Experiment 2 collected ratings on 18 instrument sounds across 8 bipolar scales. The released dataset includes raw responses, demographics, audio stimuli, acoustic features, and extraction code.
AI.conclusion: PolSeT fills a gap in open timbre research, supporting psychoacoustic research and multilingual semantic embedding model training with both qualitative and quantitative data.

title: JustDiag!: A Diagnostic Justification Engine for Accountable Root Cause Analysis
summary: Large language models can produce fluent root cause analyses, but fluent final answers alone are insufficient evidence for accountability in high-stakes operations. In real incident response, engineers need to know what evidence supported a diagnosis, which alternatives were considered, where contradictions remained, and whether the system resolved the case or preserved uncertainty. We address this gap with JustDiag, a diagnostic justification engine for RCA that maintains an explicit process state over evidence, findings, competing hypotheses, conflicts, and next checks. We evaluated the system on 66 real-world incidents using a two-layer protocol that separately scores final-answer quality and process quality. Relative to a matched control without diagnostic justification, JustDiag achieved stronger outcome and process scores, while accepting slightly lower terminal completion due to more calibrated non-closure. These results suggest that accountable RCA requires explicit diagnostic justification artifacts and process-aware evaluation, not only fluent final answers.

AI.tldr: JustDiag is a diagnostic justification engine for root cause analysis that maintains explicit process state (evidence, findings, hypotheses, conflicts, next checks) and uses a two-layer evaluation protocol scoring both final-answer quality and process quality, achieving stronger scores than matched controls without diagnostic justification.
AI.motivation: Fluent final answers from LLMs are insufficient for accountability in high-stakes incident response; engineers need evidence-supported diagnoses, considered alternatives, contradictions, and calibrated uncertainty.
AI.method: JustDiag maintains an explicit process state tracking evidence, findings, competing hypotheses, conflicts, and next checks. Evaluated on 66 real-world incidents using a two-layer protocol that separately scores final-answer quality and process quality against a matched control without diagnostic justification.
AI.result: JustDiag achieved stronger outcome and process scores compared to the baseline, while accepting slightly lower terminal completion due to more calibrated non-closure.
AI.conclusion: Accountable RCA requires explicit diagnostic justification artifacts and process-aware evaluation, not only fluent final answers.

title: Learning When to Denoise: Optimizing Asynchronous Schedules for Latent Diffusion
summary: Multi-representation diffusion models can improve visual synthesis by denoising complementary views of an image, but their performance depends critically on the asynchronous schedule that determines when each representation is denoised. We propose to learn this schedule. Our method formulates asynchronous flow matching over multiple representation spaces and uses a schedule-corrected objective that keeps each representation's local noising-time weights fixed as the schedule changes. We instantiate the schedule with a flexible parametric class that is convex and monotone by construction, and learn it using a fast joint probe with less than 1% additional training compute. On ImageNet 256x256, the learned schedule substantially improves both convergence speed and final quality under a matched 675M-parameter XL backbone. With AutoGuidance, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 1.05, matching the 800-epoch SFD-XL baseline with 4x less training. Training to 600 epochs further improves to FID 1.02, outperforming the 1B-parameter SFD-XXL result of FID 1.04 while using a smaller model. In the unguided setting, our 200-epoch model reaches FID 2.37, already below the best 800-epoch SFD-XL result (2.54) at 4x less training, and improves to FID 2.14 at 600 epochs. Code is available at https://github.com/bsq532087/LWD

AI.tldr: This paper proposes learning the asynchronous denoising schedule for multi-representation diffusion models, using a schedule-corrected objective and a flexible parametric schedule class. The method achieves state-of-the-art FID scores with up to 4x less training compute on ImageNet 256x256.
AI.motivation: Multi-representation diffusion models depend critically on the asynchronous schedule that determines when each representation is denoised, but this schedule has not been learned and is typically hand-tuned, limiting performance.
AI.method: The authors formulate asynchronous flow matching over multiple representation spaces with a schedule-corrected objective that keeps local noising-time weights fixed as the schedule changes. They instantiate the schedule with a flexible parametric class (convex and monotone by construction) and learn it using a fast joint probe requiring less than 1% additional training compute.
AI.result: On ImageNet 256x256 with a 675M-parameter XL backbone: (1) AutoGuidance at 200 epochs reaches FID 1.05 (matching 800-epoch SFD-XL with 4x less training); (2) 600 epochs further improves to FID 1.02 (outperforming 1B-parameter SFD-XXL's FID 1.04); (3) Unguided at 200 epochs reaches FID 2.37 (below best 800-epoch SFD-XL 2.54 at 4x less training), improving to FID 2.14 at 600 epochs.
AI.conclusion: Learning the asynchronous schedule substantially improves both convergence speed and final quality in multi-representation diffusion models, enabling smaller models to outperform larger baselines with significantly less training.

title: ProMUSE: Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty-guided Staged Evidential Alzheimer Disease Classification
summary: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is a fatal disorder that destroys memory and cognitive skills in the elderly population. Most treatments for AD are effective in the early stage, leading to an increasing demand for early AD diagnosis. AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data such as clinical assessments, structural Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI), and Positron Emission Tomography (PET) imaging. However, MRI and PET acquisition remain costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinical workflows. We propose ProMUSE, a Progressive Multi-modal Uncertainty Guided Staged Evidential Network that adaptively determines when additional modalities are necessary, helping reduce the overall cost of data acquisition while maintaining accuracy. ProMUSE first performs evidential classification using low-cost clinical data and quantifies uncertainty via a Dirichlet-based subjective logic model. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, ProMUSE progressively incorporates MRI or PET features, fusing modality-wise belief and uncertainty through Dempster-Shafer theory to obtain a calibrated multimodal prediction. This staged acquisition strategy enables accurate diagnosis while minimizing reliance on expensive imaging. Experiments on ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks demonstrate that ProMUSE achieves competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%, yielding substantial cost savings. These results highlight ProMUSE as a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening.

AI.tldr: ProMUSE is a progressive multimodal uncertainty-guided network that adaptively decides when to acquire costly MRI/PET data for Alzheimer's disease diagnosis, using low-cost clinical data first and only adding imaging modalities when uncertainty is high, achieving 50-90% reduction in imaging usage while maintaining accuracy.
AI.motivation: Early AD diagnosis increasingly relies on multimodal data (clinical assessments, MRI, PET), but MRI and PET acquisition are costly and not universally accessible, making full-modality inference impractical in real-world clinical workflows. There is a need for a resource-efficient solution that minimizes expensive imaging while maintaining diagnostic accuracy.
AI.method: ProMUSE uses a Dirichlet-based subjective logic model for evidential classification on low-cost clinical data, quantifying uncertainty. When uncertainty exceeds a learned threshold, it progressively incorporates MRI or PET features, fusing modality-wise belief and uncertainty via Dempster-Shafer theory for calibrated multimodal prediction.
AI.result: Experiments on ADNI, AIBL, and OASIS across CN-AD, CN-MCI, and MCI-AD tasks show competitive or superior accuracy compared to full-modality baselines while reducing MRI/PET usage by 50-90%, yielding substantial cost savings.
AI.conclusion: ProMUSE is a practical, uncertainty-aware, and resource-efficient solution for real-world AD screening that adaptively determines when additional modalities are necessary, reducing overall data acquisition cost while maintaining diagnostic accuracy.

title: ITNet: A Learnable Integral Transform That Subsumes Convolution, Attention, and Recurrence
summary: Convolutional networks, recurrent networks, and transformers each encode different inductive biases -- locality, sequential memory, and content-dependent pairwise interaction -- and have remained mathematically distinct since their inception. We show that this fragmentation reflects not a fundamental diversity in how signals should be processed, but rather incomplete views of a single underlying mathematical object: a learnable integral transform. We introduce the Integral Transform Network (ITNet), a unified architecture built around a learnable kernel that depends jointly on positions and features. This kernel is implemented as a small neural network, specifically an MLP, that models pairwise interactions, enabling the model to adapt its behavior from data. We show that convolution, self-attention (including multi-head), and autoregressive recurrence (including LSTM, GRU, S4, and Mamba) arise as special cases under appropriate parameterizations, and that ITNet is a universal approximator of continuous operators. To make this practical, we develop tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization, enabling efficient and scalable computation. A single ITNet architecture with a shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K , GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA\,v2 and NLVR2. The results demonstrate that a single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three architectural families from data.

AI.tldr: ITNet unifies convolution, attention, and recurrence under a single learnable integral transform with a kernel MLP, achieving competitive results across vision, language, and 3D tasks.
AI.motivation: Current architectures (CNNs, RNNs, Transformers) encode distinct inductive biases yet are mathematically fragmented; the authors aim to show these are not fundamentally diverse but specialized instances of a single underlying operator—a learnable integral transform.
AI.method: ITNet uses a learnable kernel implemented as an MLP that depends jointly on positions and features (pairwise interactions). Convolution, self-attention (including multi-head), and autoregressive recurrence (LSTM, GRU, S4, Mamba) are recovered as special cases under appropriate parameterizations. Efficiency is achieved via tiled kernel fusion, importance-weighted Monte Carlo integration, and learned low-rank factorization.
AI.result: A single ITNet architecture with shared operator and lightweight modality-specific encoders matches or exceeds specialized baselines on ImageNet-1K, GLUE, ModelNet40, VQA v2, and NLVR2.
AI.conclusion: A single learned interaction mechanism can recover the behavior of all three architectural families (convolutional, recurrent, transformer) from data, demonstrating architectural unification is practically feasible.

title: Segment-Level Mandarin Chinese Speech-Based Cognitive Impairment Detection via an Autoencoder with Contrastive Learning
summary:
oindent	extbf{Background and Objective:} Speech has emerged as a low-cost and non-invasive digital biomarker with considerable potential for cognitive impairment detection. However, limited labeled data and cross-dataset variability remain major challenges for robust speech-based screening systems.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Methods:} We developed a segment-level representation learning framework for speech-based cognitive impairment detection. Speech recordings were divided into short segments and converted into spectrogram representations. To improve robustness under limited-data conditions, offline and online augmentation strategies were combined with autoencoder-based representation learning and contrastive objectives to enhance discriminative latent representations.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Results:} Experiments conducted on four independent Mandarin Chinese speech datasets demonstrated stable and competitive performance in both binary and three-class classification tasks, with particularly notable improvements in the clinically challenging three-class setting. Ablation studies further supported the effectiveness of the proposed framework.
  \par
oindent	extbf{Conclusions:} The findings suggest that segment-level speech representation learning may provide a scalable and practical approach for cognitive impairment screening in resource-constrained clinical settings.

AI.tldr: A segment-level representation learning framework combining data augmentation, autoencoder-based learning, and contrastive objectives improves speech-based cognitive impairment detection across four Mandarin Chinese datasets, especially in three-class classification tasks.
AI.motivation: Speech is a low-cost, non-invasive digital biomarker for cognitive impairment detection, but limited labeled data and cross-dataset variability hinder robust screening systems.
AI.method: Speech recordings are divided into short segments, converted to spectrograms, and processed using offline/online augmentation combined with autoencoder-based representation learning and contrastive objectives to learn discriminative latent representations.
AI.result: Stable and competitive performance on four independent Mandarin Chinese datasets in both binary and three-class classification tasks, with notable improvements in the clinically challenging three-class setting; ablation studies confirmed framework effectiveness.
AI.conclusion: Segment-level speech representation learning may offer a scalable and practical approach for cognitive impairment screening in resource-constrained clinical settings.

title: OpenRath: Session-Centered Runtime State for Agent Systems
summary: Modern agent systems often suffer from fragmented runtime state: transcripts, tool effects, memory events, workspace placement, branch provenance, and replay evidence are recorded separately and become difficult to inspect or reproduce. OpenRath addresses this issue with a PyTorch-like programming model for multi-agent, multi-session systems. The analogy concerns the role of a central first-class runtime abstraction, not tensor computation. Its core abstraction is Session, the runtime value passed between agents and workflows. A Session is branchable, inspectable, replayable, backend-aware, and composable. It records conversation chunks, sandbox placement, lineage metadata, token usage, pending work, and tool evidence, while defining where memory interactions enter the runtime record. Since this state is carried by the same value used in program execution, fork, merge, and replay become explicit runtime operations rather than states reconstructed from external traces. OpenRath further defines Sandbox, Tool, Agent, Memory, Workflow, and Selector, with Selector turning control flow into runtime-routed decisions. This report presents the programming model, architecture, audited milestones, and evidence protocol. Its claims are limited to controlled runtime properties, while broad quantitative comparisons, live-provider quality, optional-backend availability, and memory quality are left for follow-on evaluation. The central thesis is that Session provides agent systems with a first-class runtime value for auditable composition.

AI.tldr: OpenRath introduces Session as a first-class runtime abstraction (analogous to PyTorch's tensor) that unifies fragmented agent state (transcripts, tool effects, memory, provenance, replay) into a single branchable, inspectable, replayable, and composable value, enabling explicit runtime operations like fork, merge, and replay rather than post-hoc reconstruction from external traces.
AI.motivation: Modern agent systems suffer from fragmented runtime state across separate components (transcripts, tool effects, memory, workspace, provenance, replay), making inspection, reproduction, and auditing difficult.
AI.method: Defines a PyTorch-like programming model where Session is the central first-class runtime value passed between agents and workflows. Session carries all runtime state (conversation chunks, sandbox placement, lineage, token usage, tool evidence, memory interactions) and supports explicit fork, merge, and replay operations. Additional abstractions include Sandbox, Tool, Agent, Memory, Workflow, and Selector for routing control flow.
AI.result: Presents the programming model, architecture, audited milestones, and evidence protocol. Claims are limited to controlled runtime properties; quantitative comparisons, live-provider quality, optional-backend availability, and memory quality are deferred to follow-on evaluation.
AI.conclusion: Session provides agent systems with a first-class runtime value for auditable composition, making runtime state inspection and reproduction explicit rather than reconstructed from external traces.

title: TeleMorpher: Toward Robust Simultaneous Motion-Location Editing
summary: Diffusion models have achieved remarkable success in image and video generation and editing. While recent studies have extended these efforts toward motion editing, simultaneously transforming both motion and location-despite its practical importance-remains largely unexplored. To better understand robust motion-location editing, we first analyze the fundamental factors that degrade its quality. Based on this analysis, we propose TeleMorpher, one of the first one-shot frameworks to the best of our knowledge, for simultaneous motion-location editing. Our approach leverages motion priors, a target motion-centric video generated from an off-the-shelf model as motion-editing guidance, and the ground truth motion to enable more controllable and precise motion-location editing. Via this, our framework works as follows: (1) we first disentangle the protagonist and the background via pre-trained segmentation and inpainting models. (2) Then, we introduce a training-free pose warping that edits the protagonist's motion with the motion prior as the guidance. (3) The result of warped motion video is directly injected into a baseline motion editor during inference, mitigating the difference between source and target motions while preserving the appearance of the source video. (4) To enhance the reliability of quantitative evaluations, we propose two new LPIPS-based metrics that measure the background consistency before and after the motion editing and the fidelity of motion editing performance via measuring the difference between the extracted protagonist's skeletons from source and target videos. Experiments with in-the-wild videos and the TaiChi dataset demonstrate that TeleMorpher achieves superior performance across both quantitative and qualitative measurements (real-human evaluation), underscoring its effectiveness.

AI.tldr: TeleMorpher is a one-shot framework for simultaneous motion-location editing in videos, using motion priors, pose warping, and injection into a baseline motion editor to transform both protagonist movement and location while preserving appearance.
AI.motivation: Simultaneously editing both motion and location in videos is practically important but remains largely unexplored. The authors first analyze the fundamental factors degrading motion-location editing quality to motivate their approach.
AI.method: (1) Disentangle protagonist and background via pre-trained segmentation/inpainting models; (2) training-free pose warping guided by motion prior; (3) inject warped motion video into baseline motion editor during inference to mitigate source-target motion differences; (4) two new LPIPS-based metrics for quantitative evaluation.
AI.result: Superior performance over baselines on in-the-wild videos and the TaiChi dataset, validated by both quantitative metrics and real-human evaluation.
AI.conclusion: TeleMorpher effectively enables robust simultaneous motion-location editing in a one-shot setting, and the proposed LPIPS-based metrics enhance reliability of quantitative evaluation in this domain.

title: cAPM: Continual AI-Assisted Pace-Mapping with Active Learning
summary: Ventricular tachycardia is a life-threatening rhythm disorder and a major cause of sudden cardiac death. Pace-mapping is a clinical procedure for identifying the intervention target during catheter ablation of VT. It requires clinicians to pace different sites in the ventricles and rapidly interpret the resulting electrocardiograms to determine where to pace next or whether a target site has been identified. Active learning AI models have been proposed to guide clinicians to the next pacing site, showing promise in reducing the number of pacing sites and improving the efficiency of pace-mapping. Existing methods require retraining each target without the ability to transfer knowledge across multiple VTs within the same patient or across patients. We introduce cAPM for continuous AI-assisted pace-mapping to capture and transfer knowledge accumulated from past pace-mapping data to reduce the number of pace-mapping data needed for future target VTs. This is made possible by a task-agnostic surrogate neural network that learns the mapping from pacing sites to 12-lead ECG morphology, an active-learning strategy that refines this surrogate model by selecting the most informative pacing site for each target, and a continual learning strategy to do so sequentially while retaining knowledge from prior targets.
