The paper aims to show both are manifestations of a single measurable quantity—the effective dimension of the quantum feature kernel—providing a principled design principle.
AI.method: The authors work with quantum-kernel vision models (quantum feature map + kernel classifier). They provide: (1) an exact decomposition of the depolarized kernel $K_p=(1-p)^2K+\tfrac{p(2-p)}{D}\mathbf{1}\mathbf{1}^\top$ where $d_{\rm eff}(K_p)\to1$; (2) a contraction result for amplitude damping; (3) a kernel-machine capacity bound; (4) a capacity/alignment risk decomposition. They verify monotone contraction empirically (not proven in general), use the depolarizing family for exact confirmation up to 12 qubits, and empirically demonstrate amplitude damping's inverted-U sweet spot yielding up to +13% accuracy improvement.
AI.result: Both entanglement structure and quantum noise are knobs that move $d_{\rm eff}$; contracting $d_{\rm eff}$ acts as ridge-like regularization in overfitting regimes. Amplitude damping noise contracts $d_{\rm eff}$ and lifts test accuracy by up to +13% along an inverted-U sweet spot; the effect sign flips between over- and under-fitting regimes. Noise injection matches an explicit spectral-filtering frontier.
AI.conclusion: Two previously unexplained empirical anecdotes—the entanglement-generalization relationship and noise-induced accuracy improvement—are organized into a single measurable principle (effective dimension $d_{\rm eff}$) for designing quantum-vision models, providing a principled spectral account that replaces grid-search heuristics.

title: HumanScale: Egocentric Human Video Can Outperform Real-Robot Data for Embodied Pretraining
summary: Embodied foundation models are expected to benefit from data scaling like large language models, but face a much tighter data bottleneck. Teleoperated real-robot trajectories remain the dominant pretraining source due to their precise action supervision and embodiment alignment, yet their scalability is limited by high collection cost, acquisition difficulty, and low behavioral and environmental diversity. These limitations have sparked interest in egocentric human video as a scalable, substantially lower-cost, and more diverse alternative for embodied model pretraining. However, its effectiveness compared to teleoperated real-robot data remains underexplored. To address this question, we conduct a systematic study comparing egocentric human video and teleoperated real-robot trajectories as pretraining data sources for embodied foundation models, under fixed post-training and validation protocols. Surprisingly, we find that egocentric data, when processed through a carefully designed filtering and labeling pipeline, is not merely a viable substitute for model pretraining but can lead to superior performance. With the same amount of pretraining data, models pretrained on egocentric data achieve a 24% lower validation loss on real-robot action prediction, as well as 52.5% and 90% higher success rates on in-distribution and out-of-distribution real-robot task execution, respectively. This finding verifies a scalable paradigm for embodied foundation models: pretrain on egocentric human video to learn diverse world representations, then adapt with a small amount of labeled real-robot data for action-space alignment. We hope this study encourages broader exploration of egocentric data and offers guidance for data quality assessment before costly robot data collection.

AI.tldr: This paper systematically compares egocentric human video and teleoperated real-robot trajectories as pretraining data for embodied foundation models. Surprisingly, models pretrained on egocentric human video (with a careful filtering/labeling pipeline) outperform those trained on real-robot data, achieving 24% lower validation loss, 52.5% higher in-distribution success rate, and 90% higher out-of-distribution success rate, validating a scalable paradigm of pretraining on human video followed by minimal robot data adaptation.
AI.motivation: Teleoperated real-robot trajectories are the dominant pretraining source for embodied foundation models but suffer from high cost, difficulty, and low diversity. Egocentric human video is a cheaper, more scalable alternative, but its effectiveness relative to real-robot data was underexplored. This study aims to fill that gap.
AI.method: Systematic comparative study under fixed post-training and validation protocols, comparing egocentric human video vs. teleoperated real-robot trajectories as pretraining data. The egocentric data is processed through a carefully designed filtering and labeling pipeline before pretraining.
AI.result: Egocentric data-pretrained models achieve 24% lower validation loss on real-robot action prediction, and 52.5% higher in-distribution and 90% higher out-of-distribution success rates on real-robot task execution, compared to models pretrained on the same amount of real-robot data.
AI.conclusion: Pretraining on egocentric human video to learn diverse world representations, then adapting with a small amount of labeled real-robot data for action-space alignment, is a scalable and effective paradigm for embodied foundation models. This encourages broader use of egocentric data and offers guidance for data quality assessment before costly robot data collection.

title: Learner-based Concept Drift Detection: Analysis and Evaluation
summary: Machine learning algorithms deployed for evolving streaming environments must handle the non-stationary data distributions, commonly referred to as concept drift. The presence of concept drift poses a major challenge for many real-world applications because it can severely degrade their predictive performance, hindering their ability to support robust decision-making. Consequently, the timely and efficient detection of drift events is critical for sustaining high accuracy over time. This study examines theoretically the concept drift characteristics and numerous drift detection algorithms across several categories. Furthermore, we evaluate their performance on both synthetic and real-world datasets exhibiting diverse streaming scenarios and drift characteristics, such as abrupt and gradual changes. This study aims to enhance understanding of the complex notion of concept drift characteristics and behavior of drift detectors, along with their applicability to diverse contexts.

AI.tldr: This paper provides a theoretical examination of concept drift characteristics and evaluates various drift detection algorithms across synthetic and real-world datasets with diverse streaming scenarios, including abrupt and gradual changes.
AI.motivation: Concept drift severely degrades predictive performance in evolving streaming environments, hindering robust decision-making; thus, timely detection of drift events is critical.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis of concept drift characteristics and drift detection algorithms across multiple categories, with empirical evaluations on synthetic and real-world datasets featuring diverse drift types (abrupt, gradual).
AI.result: Performance evaluation of drift detectors under various streaming scenarios and drift characteristics.
AI.conclusion: The study enhances understanding of concept drift characteristics, drift detector behavior, and their applicability to diverse contexts.

title: SARLO-80: Worldwide Slant SAR Language Optic Dataset 80cm
summary: Multimodal foundation models have advanced rapidly thanks to large optical benchmarks, but comparable resources for synthetic aperture radar (SAR) remain limited. Existing SAR--optical datasets largely rely on low-resolution, intensity-only Ground Range Detected~(GRD) products and do not preserve complex-valued SAR measurements or native acquisition geometry, which restricts physically grounded multimodal learning. In particular, large-scale public datasets combining very-high-resolution (VHR) SAR SLC, aligned optical imagery, and natural-language descriptions are still lacking. We present a VHR SAR--optical--text dataset built from open-access Umbra spotlight acquisitions distributed as Sensor Independent Complex Data (SICD). From around 2,500 worldwide scenes (VV/HH, 20cm--2m native resolution), we standardize all SAR data to an 80cm slant-range grid via band-limited FFT resampling and tile the imagery into 1024 by 1024 patches. For each SAR patch, we retrieve a high-resolution optical tile and warp it into the SAR grid using local coordinate correspondences for local pixel-level alignment. We further generate three caption variants (SHORT/MID/LONG) per sample to support vision--language training and evaluation. Our dataset contains 119,566 triplets (complex and amplitude slant-range SAR patch, aligned optical patch, natural-language description) covering 257 locations across 72 countries and a broad range of land types and infrastructures. We release fixed train/validation/test splits and the full preprocessing and baseline code to enable reproducible benchmarks for multimodal alignment on cross-modal retrieval and conditional generation in native SAR geometry. The dataset is publicly available on the Hugging Face Hub at https://huggingface.co/datasets/ONERA/SARLO-80.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SARLO-80, a large-scale VHR SAR SLC–optical–text dataset built from Umbra spotlight SICD data. It contains 119,566 triplets (complex/amplitude slant-range SAR patches, aligned optical patches, and natural-language captions) across 257 locations in 72 countries, standardized to 80cm slant-range resolution, enabling physically grounded multimodal learning for SAR.
AI.motivation: Existing SAR–optical datasets rely on low-resolution, intensity-only GRD products that discard complex-valued SAR measurements and native acquisition geometry, limiting physically grounded multimodal learning. Large-scale public datasets combining VHR SAR SLC with aligned optical imagery and natural-language descriptions are lacking.
AI.method: Built from ~2,500 Umbra spotlight SICD scenes (VV/HH, 20cm–2m native resolution). SAR data standardized to 80cm slant-range grid via band-limited FFT resampling, tiled into 1024×1024 patches. High-resolution optical tiles are retrieved and warped into SAR grid using local coordinate correspondences for pixel-level alignment. Three caption variants (SHORT/MID/LONG) are generated per sample.
AI.result: 119,566 triplets (complex + amplitude SAR patches, aligned optical patches, natural-language descriptions), 257 locations across 72 countries, diverse land types and infrastructures. Fixed train/validation/test splits and full preprocessing code are publicly released.
AI.conclusion: SARLO-80 fills the gap of large-scale VHR SAR SLC–optical–text benchmarks in native SAR geometry, enabling reproducible multimodal alignment research including cross-modal retrieval and conditional generation.

title: Boundary Embedding Shaping with Adaptive Contrastive Learning for Graph Structural Disentanglement
summary: Graph neural networks (GNNs) excel at aggregating neighbor information for classification, yet their performance is hindered by graph structural entanglement, where spurious correlations from semantically irrelevant neighbors contaminate node embeddings. This challenge is most acute for nodes near class boundaries in the embedding space, where amplified structural noise blurs decision boundaries and destabilizes predictions. Existing robust GNN methods largely treat all nodes uniformly, ignoring boundary vulnerabilities. In this paper, to improve classification performance, we tackle graph structural disentanglement by identifying boundary-region entanglement as the primary bottleneck and propose Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning GNN plug-in module that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries with minimal model parameter perturbation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that BES consistently improves boundary discrimination and outperforms existing leading methods. Notably, BES boosts GCN performance by an average of 3.3% in node classification (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and achieves superior accuracy in link prediction.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies graph structural entanglement as a key bottleneck for GNN performance, particularly for boundary nodes, and proposes Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES), an adaptive contrastive learning plug-in module that suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries. BES consistently improves node classification (e.g., 3.3% average boost for GCN, up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and link prediction over existing robust GNN methods.
AI.motivation: GNNs suffer from graph structural entanglement where spurious correlations from semantically irrelevant neighbors contaminate node embeddings, with boundary nodes being most vulnerable. Existing robust methods treat all nodes uniformly and fail to address boundary-specific vulnerabilities.
AI.method: Boundary Embedding Shaping (BES) — an adaptive contrastive learning plug-in module for GNNs that selectively suppresses spurious structural noise at decision boundaries with minimal model parameter perturbation.
AI.result: BES consistently improves boundary discrimination and outperforms existing leading methods. It boosts GCN node classification by 3.3% on average (up to 5.0% on WikiCS) and achieves superior accuracy in link prediction.
AI.conclusion: Addressing boundary-region entanglement is key to improving GNN classification performance, and BES effectively does so as a lightweight plug-in module.

title: VisDom: Sparse Novel View Synthesis with Visible Domain Constraint
summary: Sparse novel view synthesis (NVS) remains challenging due to the ambiguity of recovering 3D geometry from few input views. While NeRF- and Gaussian Splatting (GS)-based methods perform well with dense supervision, they often overfit in sparse settings, producing floating artifacts and inconsistent geometry. Silhouette consistency is commonly used as a regularizer, but it remains insufficient, as silhouette-consistent regions can extend beyond the true object geometry. We introduce VisDom, a learning-free geometric constraint that augments classical carving-based visual hull reconstruction by enforcing a minimum multi-view visibility requirement. Specifically, we define a visible domain as the subset of 3D space observed by at least $K$ views and use it as an additional filtering criterion on top of standard silhouette-based reconstruction. This provides a stronger spatial prior in sparse-view settings. We integrate VisDom into both implicit (NeRF) and explicit (GS) pipelines by restricting volumetric sampling and guiding Gaussian placement during optimization. Experiments on three challenging datasets show consistent improvements in sparse-view NVS, enabling high-quality object-centric reconstruction from as few as four input images. Our method is domain-agnostic, requires only silhouettes, and introduces no learned parameters, making it a simple complement to existing approaches. Applying VisDom on top of GaussianObject further improves performance on Omni3D and MipNeRF360, while matching or surpassing it at 22 $\times$ lower training cost.

AI.tldr: VisDom introduces a learning-free geometric constraint that enforces a minimum multi-view visibility requirement on top of silhouette-based reconstruction, improving sparse-view novel view synthesis in both NeRF and Gaussian Splatting pipelines with no learned parameters.
AI.motivation: Sparse novel view synthesis suffers from geometry ambiguity and overfitting when using few input views. Existing silhouette consistency regularization is insufficient because silhouette-consistent regions can extend beyond actual object geometry, leading to floating artifacts and inconsistent geometry.
AI.method: VisDom defines a "visible domain" as the subset of 3D space observed by at least K views, enforcing this as a filtering criterion on top of standard silhouette-based visual hull reconstruction. It integrates into NeRF by restricting volumetric sampling and into Gaussian Splatting by guiding Gaussian placement during optimization.
AI.result: Consistent improvements on three challenging datasets for sparse-view NVS from as few as 4 input images. Applying VisDom on top of GaussianObject improves performance on Omni3D and MipNeRF360, matching or surpassing it at 22× lower training cost.
AI.conclusion: VisDom is a simple, domain-agnostic, learning-free complement to existing NVS approaches that requires only silhouettes and introduces no learned parameters, providing stronger spatial priors in sparse-view settings.

title: Integrating national forest inventory, airborne lidar, and satellite imagery for wall-to-wall mapping of forest structure with computer vision
summary: Remote sensing is increasingly relied upon to deliver actionable science for forest and wildfire risk management across large landscapes. Wall-to-wall, annually updated maps are a persistent need for effective forest management. Many planning systems and data collections combine disparate data sources with different purposes, vintages, and prediction quality, which leads to confounding behavior in operational planning systems. We introduce the VibrantForests framework, developed and applied to map forest attributes and provide a coherent foundation for effective forest and wildfire planning. VibrantForests includes a satellite-based forest structure model trained on lidar-derived samples and applied across the contiguous United States to concurrently generate estimates of canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter at 10-meter resolution. We demonstrate predictive capability spanning the full spectrum of forest conditions ranging from sparse-canopy/low-biomass to dense-canopy/high-biomass. Results show that our model extends the range at which saturation is commonly encountered in comparable passive-sensor models, and reduces regression-to-mean behavior that commonly produces overestimation of forest attributes in small/sparse conditions and underestimation in large/dense conditions. The VibrantForests framework addresses a key limitation in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent wall-to-wall estimates of management-relevant attributes at annual cadence and 10m resolution.

AI.tldr: VibrantForests is a satellite-based framework that generates coherent, wall-to-wall, annually updated 10m-resolution maps of five key forest attributes across the contiguous US, using lidar-trained models that reduce saturation and regression-to-mean biases.
AI.motivation: Current forest and wildfire planning relies on disparate data sources with different vintages and quality, leading to confounding behavior in operational planning systems. There is a persistent need for coherent, wall-to-wall, annually updated forest attribute maps at high resolution.
AI.method: A satellite-based forest structure model trained on lidar-derived samples, applied across the contiguous US at 10m resolution to concurrently estimate canopy cover, canopy height, aboveground live tree biomass, basal area, and quadratic mean diameter.
AI.result: The model extends the saturation range compared to passive-sensor models and reduces regression-to-mean behavior, avoiding overestimation in sparse conditions and underestimation in dense conditions, while demonstrating capability across the full forest condition spectrum.
AI.conclusion: VibrantForests addresses key limitations in large-area forest and wildfire planning by delivering coherent wall-to-wall estimates of management-relevant attributes at annual cadence and 10m resolution.

title: The FID Lottery: Quantifying Hidden Randomness in Generative-Model Evaluation
summary: The Frechet Inception Distance (FID) is the de facto arbiter of image generation, yet most papers report just a single number from a single trained model using a single sampling seed. How reproducible is that number if we retrain the model, or merely resample from it? In this paper, we treat FID as a random variable on a two-axis panel of training and generation seeds, and measure its variance directly on several hundred SiT networks trained on class-conditional ImageNet 256x256. We report surprising findings: (a) Retraining the model using the same recipe with a different seed moves FID 3.2x more (in Inception feature space) than redrawing samples from a fixed network. (b) That gap is driven by three factors: random initialisation, data ordering, and the per-step Gaussian noise of the flow-matching loss. (c) Increasing compute or model size barely tightens the spread, holding the FID coefficient of variation (CoV) inside a 1-2% band. (d) Per-cell classifier-free-guidance tuning halves the spread but reshuffles which seeds work best, and a lucky training seed reaches the same FID with up to 2x less compute than an unlucky one. Based on these findings, we recommend a new FID evaluation protocol: evaluate under per-cell optimal guidance, treat any FID gap below the empirically measured ~1.3% CoV as inconclusive, and report an error bar over several training seeds rather than a single FID number.

AI.tldr: FID variance is primarily driven by training seeds (3.2x more than sampling seeds), with a ~1.3% coefficient of variation; the paper proposes a new evaluation protocol with per-cell optimal guidance, reporting error bars over multiple training seeds.
AI.motivation: To investigate the reproducibility of FID scores, since the community typically reports a single FID number despite it being a random variable dependent on both training and sampling seeds.
AI.method: Measured FID variance across hundreds of SiT networks trained on class-conditional ImageNet 256x256, systematically varying training seeds and sampling seeds, and analyzing the contributions of random initialization, data ordering, and flow-matching Gaussian noise.
AI.result: (a) Retraining changes FID 3.2x more than resampling; (b) variance stems from initialization, data ordering, and noise; (c) increasing compute/model size barely reduces spread (CoV 1-2%); (d) per-cell CFG tuning halves spread but reshuffles seed rankings; lucky seeds achieve same FID with 2x less compute.
AI.conclusion: Recommend evaluating under per-cell optimal guidance, treating FID gaps below ~1.3% CoV as inconclusive, and reporting error bars over multiple training seeds instead of a single number.

title: Shifting-based Optimizable Linear Relaxations for General Activation Functions
summary: The use of neural networks (NNs) is rapidly increasing, including in safety- and security-critical domains. To provide formal guarantees about NN behavior, many verification methods rely on optimizable linear relaxations of activation functions. However, existing techniques depend on hand-crafted relaxations for each activation function. Extension to state-of-the-art activation functions therefore requires substantial manual effort. In contrast, our approach SLiR (Shifting-based Linear Relaxations) is broadly applicable, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or a set of critical points. SLiR parameterizes relaxations by their slope and computes the corresponding offset via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper and lower bounds over the input domain, enabling efficient optimization while maintaining correctness. Our experiments show that SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of practical activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.

AI.tldr: SLiR is a novel method for generating sound linear relaxations of activation functions for NN verification that works with any Lipschitz-continuous activation function, producing tighter bounds and enabling up to 7.8x more verified properties compared to hand-crafted approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing NN verification methods rely on hand-crafted linear relaxations for each activation function, requiring substantial manual effort to support new activation functions. A general, automated approach is needed.
AI.method: SLiR parameterizes relaxations by slope and computes offset via a shifting procedure that ensures sound upper/lower bounds over the input domain, requiring only a Lipschitz constant or set of critical points.
AI.result: SLiR produces tight relaxations across a wide range of activation functions and enables verification of up to 7.8x more properties compared to state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: SLiR is a broadly applicable and efficient approach for generating linear relaxations of activation functions, eliminating the need for hand-crafted designs while improving verification performance.

title: CalTennis: Large Multi-View Tennis Video Dataset and Benchmark of Monocular-to-3D Pose Estimation
summary: The Caltech Tennis Dataset (CalTennis) is a large-scale video benchmark for evaluating monocular-to-3D pose estimation in the wild. CalTennis comprises over 11 million frames (51 hours) of tennis practice and match play from 40 players, captured with 2-6 synchronized cameras at 60 Hz. It is 10 times larger than existing in-the-wild human motion video datasets and 3 times larger than existing MOCAP-ground-truthed datasets, and it is the first large-scale benchmark to provide synchronized multi-view recordings of expert athletic motion. The multi-view setup enables inexpensive, label-free evaluation of monocular-to-3D pose estimation algorithms. We describe a simple, standardized protocol that enables data collection without specialized equipment or expertise, along with fully automated video calibration and synchronization. Benchmarking state-of-the-art monocular-to-3D pose methods on CalTennis, we find that while 3D joint angle recovery is now quite accurate, all models struggle to estimate depth and foot contact consistently. We further propose two novel performance metrics, footwork and stability, as well as qualitatively study body shape inconsistency. These metrics expose previously underexplored failure modes and point to concrete opportunities for improvement in pose estimation and action analysis.

AI.tldr: CalTennis is a large-scale multi-view tennis video benchmark (11M+ frames, 40 players) for evaluating monocular-to-3D pose estimation, which reveals that while joint angles are accurate, depth and foot contact estimation remain challenging.
AI.motivation: Existing in-the-wild human motion datasets lack sufficient scale, multi-view recordings of expert athletic motion, and standardized low-cost protocols for label-free evaluation of monocular-to-3D pose estimation.
AI.method: The authors collected 51 hours of tennis footage using 2-6 synchronized cameras at 60 Hz, implemented a standardized protocol with automated calibration and synchronization, and proposed two novel metrics (footwork and stability) plus qualitative body shape inconsistency analysis.
AI.result: CalTennis is 10× larger than existing in-the-wild motion video datasets and 3× larger than MOCAP-ground-truthed datasets. Benchmarking shows 3D joint angle recovery is accurate, but depth estimation and foot contact consistency are poor across state-of-the-art models.
AI.conclusion: The new metrics (footwork, stability) expose underexplored failure modes in pose estimation, highlighting concrete opportunities for improving depth perception and foot contact modeling in monocular-to-3D methods.

title: Recurrent neural networks approximate continuous functions
summary: Classical approximation theorems ask for a new neural network whenever the target accuracy is improved. This paper studies the opposite possibility: can the network be chosen once and for all, and can accuracy be bought only by letting it run longer? We prove that this is possible for every continuous function on [-1,1]. More precisely, each such function is uniformly approximated by the time evolution of a single ReLU recurrent neural network with fixed weights and fixed hidden dimension. The mechanism behind the construction is a new intermediate model, the Turing machine with neural units (TMNU). This model retains the algorithmic freedom needed to implement polynomial approximation schemes, while remaining rigid enough to be simulated by RNNs with explicit bounds on hidden dimension and weight magnitude. The resulting convergence rates reflect the underlying polynomial approximation rates. We complement the construction with minimax lower bounds showing that runtime is not merely a proof artifact, but an unavoidable resource in this fixed-network approximation paradigm.

AI.tldr: This paper proves that any continuous function on [-1,1] can be uniformly approximated by a single fixed ReLU recurrent neural network (with fixed weights and hidden dimension) simply by letting it run longer, using a novel intermediate model called Turing machine with neural units (TMNU), and provides matching lower bounds showing runtime is unavoidable.
AI.motivation: Classical approximation theorems require a new network whenever target accuracy improves; this paper investigates whether a single fixed network can achieve arbitrary accuracy by increasing runtime instead.
AI.method: The authors introduce an intermediate model called Turing machine with neural units (TMNU), which retains algorithmic flexibility for polynomial approximation while being simulatable by RNNs with bounded hidden dimension and weight magnitude. They use TMNU to construct fixed-weight ReLU RNNs that approximate functions via time evolution.
AI.result: Every continuous function on [-1,1] can be uniformly approximated by a single fixed ReLU RNN with fixed weights and hidden dimension, with convergence rates matching polynomial approximation rates. Minimax lower bounds confirm runtime is an unavoidable resource.
AI.conclusion: The fixed-network approximation paradigm is feasible for continuous functions, but runtime is an essential and irreducible resource in this setting.

title: SSD: Spatially Speculative Decoding Accelerates Autoregressive Image Generation
summary: Autoregressive models excel in visual generation by treating images as 1D sequences of discrete tokens, mirroring language modeling. However, this flattening discards the intrinsic 2D spatial locality of visual signals, creating severe computational bottlenecks during inference. We introduce Spatially Speculative Decoding (SSD), a framework that aligns the predictive objective with the natural geometry of images. Rather than predicting only the immediate next token in a 1D sequence, our model simultaneously predicts the adjacent horizontal token and the token directly below it. By capitalizing on this 2D spatial correlation, spatially speculative decoding overcomes the memory wall in visual inference. Our approach accelerates autoregressive image generation by up to 13.3x while maintaining high fidelity on DPG-Bench and GenEval. Our results suggest that respecting the underlying geometry of vision unlocks massive computational efficiencies, paving the way for real-time, high-resolution autoregressive generative models.

AI.tldr: Spatially Speculative Decoding (SSD) accelerates autoregressive image generation by up to 13.3x by predicting adjacent 2D tokens simultaneously instead of sequential 1D tokens, leveraging spatial locality to overcome memory bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive visual models treat images as flattened 1D sequences, discarding the intrinsic 2D spatial locality of visual signals, which creates severe computational bottlenecks during inference.
AI.method: SSD modifies the predictive objective to simultaneously predict the adjacent horizontal token and the token directly below (2D neighbors), rather than only the immediate next token in a 1D sequence, exploiting 2D spatial correlation.
AI.result: Achieves up to 13.3x acceleration in autoregressive image generation while maintaining high fidelity on DPG-Bench and GenEval benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Respecting the underlying 2D geometry of vision unlocks massive computational efficiencies, enabling real-time, high-resolution autoregressive generative models.

title: Quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold networks for PDEs
summary: We develop QCPIKAN, the first quantum-classical physics-informed Kolmogorov-Arnold network designed to solve partial differential equations (PDEs). Built upon Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits, this hybrid framework embeds physical constraints into the training loss to enforce physical consistency. Our theoretical investigations grounded in approximation theory prove that this design accelerates high-frequency error convergence to an exponential rate and effectively mitigates numerical dispersion. We validate the framework across three typical seepage scenarios in porous media, including single-phase flow, component transport and two-phase flow. Compared with existing quantum-classical physics-informed neural networks, QCPIKAN achieves superior performance in global prediction accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking and displacement front localization. This work provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs.

AI.tldr: QCPIKAN is a novel quantum-classical hybrid framework using Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers and parameterized quantum circuits to solve PDEs with physical constraints, proving exponential convergence for high-frequency errors and outperforming existing methods in seepage flow scenarios.
AI.motivation: To develop a hybrid quantum-classical network that embeds physical constraints for solving PDEs, with theoretical guarantees on error convergence and numerical dispersion mitigation.
AI.method: Combines Chebyshev-polynomial KAN layers with parameterized quantum circuits in a physics-informed hybrid framework; loss function incorporates physical constraints; theoretical analysis uses approximation theory to prove exponential convergence rate for high-frequency errors.
AI.result: Validated on three seepage scenarios (single-phase flow, component transport, two-phase flow); demonstrates superior global prediction accuracy, local error control, dynamic evolution tracking, and displacement front localization compared to existing quantum-classical PINNs.
AI.conclusion: QCPIKAN provides a robust and efficient alternative for solving complex PDEs, with theoretical guarantees and strong empirical performance in porous media flow problems.

title: Current World Models Lack a Persistent State Core
summary: World models are increasingly regarded as a decisive step toward artificial general intelligence, yet modeling the physical world demands more than rendering convincing frames on demand: it requires an internal world state that keeps evolving over time, decoupled from observation, so that objects endure and events run to their conclusions whether or not a camera is watching, much as the moon holds to its orbit when no one is looking. This requirement is a blind spot of existing benchmarks, which reward surface properties such as fidelity, motion, and camera controllability while never asking whether a generated world keeps evolving once it is unobserved. We introduce \textbf{WRBench}, the first systematic diagnostic benchmark that treats camera motion as an intervention on observability and resolves evaluation into a human-calibrated chain that asks whether the camera executes the requested interaction, whether the scene stays continuous and identifiable while in view, and whether a returning target remains consistent with the event that was set in motion. Across 9{,}600 videos from 23 models spanning four control paradigms, one finding proves stubborn: current systems maintain the observed world as a tracking shot, resuming a returning target in the state at which it was abandoned rather than advancing the event while it went unseen. Because this failure recurs across control paradigms, model families, and increments of scale, robust world-state evolution does not follow from cleaner imagery, tighter control, richer geometric priors, or sheer parameter count We therefore argue that the stability of the physical state kernel and the consistency of worldlines under viewpoint intervention should become first-class objectives of world-model design, so that a world model captures how the world will unfold rather than how the next frame appears.

AI.tldr: WRBench is a benchmark revealing that current world models fail to maintain consistent world-state evolution when unobserved (e.g., camera moves away), instead returning objects to their last observed state rather than advancing events during occlusion.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for world models focus on surface-level properties (fidelity, motion, camera controllability) and never test whether generated worlds continue to evolve when unobserved. The authors argue that true world modeling requires objects and events to persist and progress regardless of observability.
AI.method: WRBench treats camera motion as an intervention on observability, using a human-calibrated evaluation chain: (1) whether the camera executes requested interaction, (2) whether the scene stays continuous while in view, (3) whether a returning target remains consistent with the event set in motion during unobserved periods. Evaluated on 9,600 videos from 23 models across 4 control paradigms.
AI.result: All current systems fail to maintain world-state evolution when unobserved—they resume a returning target in the state at which it was abandoned rather than advancing the event. This failure is consistent across control paradigms, model families, and scales.
AI.conclusion: Robust world-state evolution does not follow from cleaner imagery, tighter control, richer geometric priors, or larger parameter counts. The stability of the physical state kernel and consistency of worldlines under viewpoint intervention should become first-class objectives of world-model design.

title: Constrained hybrid modelling to predict microbial dynamics and organic matter turnover in soil systems
summary: Soil microorganisms control organic matter cycling and largely determine how soil systems can cope with and mitigate climate change and environmental threats. Representing microbial dynamics in process-based soil models is therefore critical to predict carbon cycling in soils, albeit highly challenging to inform from data. One promising approach to improve their parametrisation is the integration of genomic data, yet modelling the complex and unknown relationship between genomes and the processes the microbes are driving is an unsolved problem. In this work, we present the first hybrid modeling framework for deriving biokinetic parameter values of a process-based soil organic matter turnover model from metagenome-inferred functional traits based on DNA sequencing data. Our model predicts biokinetic parameters of the process-based model from genomic trait data with a neural network and integrates constraints from ecological theory and literature to ensure realistic behavior, even of non-observed state variables. We evaluate our method on synthetic genomic trait datasets of varying complexity and on real data, showing that our approach improves performance over multiple baselines and learns the dynamics of unmeasurable components of the process-based model effectively, even for small training datasets.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a hybrid modeling framework that uses a neural network to predict biokinetic parameters of a process-based soil organic matter model from metagenome-inferred functional traits, integrating ecological constraints to ensure realistic predictions even with limited data.
AI.motivation: Soil microorganisms drive organic matter cycling, but representing microbial dynamics in process-based soil models is challenging due to the complex, unknown relationship between genomes and microbial processes. Integrating genomic data into these models could improve parameterization, but a solution for modeling this relationship is lacking.
AI.method: The authors develop a hybrid model combining a neural network that predicts biokinetic parameters of a process-based soil organic matter turnover model from metagenome-inferred functional traits (DNA sequencing data), with ecological theory and literature constraints integrated to ensure realistic behavior of unobserved state variables.
AI.result: The method outperforms multiple baselines on both synthetic and real genomic trait datasets, effectively learns dynamics of unmeasurable process-based model components, and performs well even with small training datasets.
AI.conclusion: This hybrid modeling framework successfully bridges genomic data and process-based soil models, offering a promising pathway to improve carbon cycling predictions by integrating metagenomic information while maintaining ecological realism.

title: Thinking in Boxes: 3D Editing in Real Images Made Easy
summary: Text and 2D-conditioning interfaces provide weak, ambiguous control over spatial transformations in image editing -- particularly under large object motions and camera changes. Prior work has used 3D primitives such as boxes, but only as loose conditioning signals indicating approximate object location rather than specifying the transformation. We instead use 3D boxes as structured specifications: the user provides the input and output boxes of the edit, casting editing as a well-posed geometry problem. This ``thinking in boxes'' interface, where each box face is color-coded to convey 3D orientation, gives precise control over translation, rotation, scaling, and viewpoint changes in real images while preserving scene and object identity, and recovering previously unseen object regions. To ground transformations in scene appearance, we introduce a depth-aligned planar floor as a global reference frame, shaded with depth-aware cues. Conditioned on this structure, an image generator produces consistent results under large transformations. Trained in two stages -- on synthetic multi-object scenes and a small set of real-world videos from Objectron -- the system generalizes to complex, in-the-wild real images. Our method operates directly on real photographs and substantially outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods on large 3D edits.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "thinking in boxes" — using 3D boxes with color-coded faces as structured specifications for precise control over spatial transformations (translation, rotation, scaling, viewpoint changes) in real image editing, achieving state-of-the-art performance on large 3D edits.
AI.motivation: Text and 2D conditioning provide weak, ambiguous control over spatial transformations in image editing, especially under large object motions and camera changes. Prior 3D box-based methods only use boxes as loose conditioning signals rather than precise transformation specifications.
AI.method: The method uses 3D boxes as structured specifications where users provide input and output boxes for editing. It introduces a depth-aligned planar floor as a global reference frame with depth-aware shading cues. Training is done in two stages: synthetic multi-object scenes and a small set of real-world videos from Objectron.
AI.result: The system generalizes to complex in-the-wild real images and substantially outperforms recent state-of-the-art methods on large 3D edits.
AI.conclusion: Using 3D boxes as structured specifications with a depth-aligned floor reference enables precise control over large spatial transformations in real image editing while preserving scene and object identity and recovering unseen object regions.

title: Critical Percolation as a Synthetic Data Model for Interpretability
comment: 21 pages, 10 figures, accepted to the Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop at ICML 2026
summary: Neural networks learn features that reflect the hierarchical, multi-scale structure of natural data. Synthetic datasets used to evaluate interpretability methods typically lack this structure, limiting their value as realistic toy models. To close this gap, we introduce a family of synthetic datasets consisting of hierarchical functions defined on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in a high-dimensional data space. The percolation data consists of sparse, low-dimensional fractal clusters with a power-law size distribution. Latent variables modeling a taxonomic hierarchy generate each data point's target value. The data model is analytically tractable with known critical exponents that fix its properties without requiring hyperparameter tuning. We leverage a mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence to propose an almost linear-time algorithm to jointly sample a random tree and its hierarchical latent decomposition, enabling data generation at arbitrary scale. Using probing experiments, we find that the model's ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations. Together, sparsity, self-similarity, power-law statistics, and analytical tractability make critical percolation a principled testbed for interpretability research.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces synthetic datasets based on critical percolation clusters that mimic the hierarchical, multi-scale structure of natural data, enabling principled evaluation of interpretability methods for neural networks.
AI.motivation: Existing synthetic datasets used for evaluating interpretability methods lack the hierarchical, multi-scale structure inherent in natural data, making them unrealistic toy models.
AI.method: The authors construct hierarchical functions on critical mean-field percolation clusters embedded in high-dimensional space, using latent variables that model a taxonomic hierarchy to generate target values. They propose an almost linear-time algorithm leveraging the mapping between percolation clusters, random trees, and additive coalescence for scalable data generation.
AI.result: The model's ground-truth latent variables can be linearly decoded from neural network activations in probing experiments. The data exhibits sparsity, self-similarity, and power-law statistics without requiring hyperparameter tuning.
AI.conclusion: Critical percolation provides a principled, analytically tractable testbed for interpretability research due to its hierarchical structure, known critical exponents, and computational efficiency.

title: UNIEGO: Proxies as Mediators for Unified Egocentric Video Representation Learning
summary: Egocentric video understanding is inherently limited by the narrow perspective of wearable cameras: a single viewpoint, a single modality, a single model cannot capture the full richness of human action. We argue that a truly expressive egocentric representation must subsume complementary knowledge across viewpoints, modalities, and foundation model representations, yet remain deployable from egocentric video alone. To this end, we introduce a hierarchical multi-teacher distillation framework that produces UNIEGO, a unified egocentric encoder trained with nine teachers spanning ego-exo viewpoints, RGB, depth, and skeleton modalities, and four foundation models. Rather than distilling directly from heterogeneous teachers whose incompatible architectures and feature geometries induce conflicting gradients, our framework interposes a layer of representation-specific Proxy models that translate diverse teacher knowledge into a homogeneous egocentric space. A second distillation stage, Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD), then adaptively selects, for each training sample, the subset of proxies that are both correct and confident, distilling exclusively from reliable supervision and suppressing erroneous signals. SPD is further stabilized by initializing UNIEGO as a learned convex combination of proxy parameters, placing the unified model in a well-conditioned region of the loss landscape before distillation begins. UNIEGO achieves state-of-the-art performance across three egocentric video understanding tasks - action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation on three challenging ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines and demonstrating that structured, proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer and more discriminative egocentric representations.

AI.tldr: UNIEGO is a unified egocentric encoder trained via hierarchical multi-teacher distillation from nine teachers (ego-exo views, RGB, depth, skeleton, four foundation models). It uses Proxy models to translate heterogeneous teacher knowledge into a homogeneous space, and Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD) to adaptively select reliable supervision. It achieves SOTA on action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation across three ego-exo benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Egocentric video understanding is limited by narrow perspective of wearable cameras (single viewpoint, modality, model). The authors argue that a truly expressive egocentric representation must integrate complementary knowledge across viewpoints, modalities, and foundation models, while remaining deployable from egocentric video alone.
AI.method: A hierarchical multi-teacher distillation framework: (1) First stage trains representation-specific Proxy models to translate diverse teacher knowledge (from 9 teachers across ego-exo viewpoints, RGB/depth/skeleton modalities, 4 foundation models) into a homogeneous egocentric space. (2) Second stage uses Selective Proxy Distillation (SPD), adaptively selecting for each sample the subset of proxies that are both correct and confident, distilling only from reliable supervision. (3) UNIEGO is initialized as a learned convex combination of proxy parameters to place the model in a well-conditioned region of the loss landscape.
AI.result: State-of-the-art performance on action recognition, video retrieval, and action segmentation across three challenging ego-exo benchmarks, outperforming naive multi-teacher distillation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Structured, proxy-mediated knowledge transfer yields richer and more discriminative egocentric representations. The framework effectively addresses the heterogeneity challenge in multi-teacher distillation.

title: On the Variance of Temporal Difference Learning and its Reduction Using Control Variates
summary: We analyze the variance of temporal difference (TD) learning using the phased setting with tabular representation, and show that one of the mechanisms behind its ability to reduce variance is by effectively aggregating over a larger number of independent trajectories. Based on this insight, we demonstrate that (1) the variance of TD is asymptotically bounded from above by Monte Carlo (MC) estimators, and (2) shorter horizon updates incurs less variance for a fixed number of samples. Beyond TD, we show that Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE), a method for estimating the advantage function, can be seen as a type of regression-adjusted control variate, which achieves a tighter bound on the variance compared to TD in the large-sample limit. Finally, we numerically illustrate the behaviors of these estimators with carefully designed environments.

AI.tldr: This paper theoretically analyzes the variance of TD learning in the tabular phased setting, showing TD reduces variance by aggregating over more trajectories. It proves TD variance is asymptotically bounded above by MC estimators, and that shorter horizons reduce variance. The paper also shows DAE is a regression-adjusted control variate achieving tighter variance bounds than TD, with numerical validations.
AI.motivation: To theoretically understand the variance properties of temporal difference (TD) learning, particularly the mechanisms behind its variance reduction compared to Monte Carlo methods, and to analyze the variance of related advantage estimation methods.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis using the phased setting with tabular representation, deriving asymptotic variance bounds for TD learning and comparing them to Monte Carlo estimators. The paper also analyzes Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) as a regression-adjusted control variate.
AI.result: (1) TD variance is asymptotically bounded above by MC estimators; (2) shorter horizon updates incur less variance for a fixed number of samples; (3) DAE achieves a tighter variance bound than TD in the large-sample limit.
AI.conclusion: TD learning reduces variance by effectively aggregating over multiple independent trajectories, and DAE further improves upon TD via a regression-adjusted control variate mechanism, providing tighter variance bounds.

title: TimeProVe: Propose, then Verify for Efficient Long Video Temporal Reasoning in Activities of Daily Living
summary: Long Video Question Answering (LVQA) requires identifying sparse, query-relevant evidence within hours-long untrimmed videos. Existing approaches either process videos densely with large vision-language models (VLMs), incurring prohibitive computational cost, or rely on sparse caption-based reasoning, which often misses temporally localized and motion-centric evidence. We introduce TimeProVe, a cost-efficient hybrid framework for temporally grounded reasoning in long videos. TimeProVe first employs lightweight modules to generate action-grounded answer--evidence hypotheses and subsequently invokes an expensive VLM only for targeted verification. The core of our framework lies in the Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module, which converts temporally localized actions into query-conditioned candidate answers and supporting evidence windows through lightweight LLM reasoning. We further introduce OpenTSUBench (OTB), an open-ended benchmark designed to evaluate temporally grounded reasoning in real-world Activities of Daily Living (ADL) scenarios. Experiments show that TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OTB by 7.3%, while reducing VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%. Furthermore, without explicit temporal grounding training, TimeProVe achieves competitive performance on Charades-STA, and reaches state-of-the-art results when enhanced with grounding VLMs.

AI.tldr: TimeProVe is a hybrid framework for Long Video QA that uses lightweight modules to generate action-grounded hypotheses and invokes expensive VLMs only for targeted verification, achieving superior performance while reducing VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%.
AI.motivation: Existing LVQA methods are either computationally prohibitive (dense VLM processing) or miss temporally localized/motion-centric evidence (sparse caption-based reasoning). A cost-efficient solution for temporally grounded reasoning is needed.
AI.method: TimeProVe uses an Action-based Candidate Evidence (ACE) module to convert temporally localized actions into query-conditioned answer/evidence hypotheses via lightweight LLM reasoning, then invokes an expensive VLM only for targeted verification. Also introduces OpenTSUBench (OTB) for evaluating temporally grounded reasoning in ADL scenarios.
AI.result: TimeProVe outperforms the strongest baseline on OTB by 7.3%, reduces VLM calls by 75% and inference cost by 93%. Achieves competitive results on Charades-STA without explicit temporal grounding training, and SOTA when enhanced with grounding VLMs.
AI.conclusion: The hybrid approach of lightweight hypothesis generation + targeted expensive verification is effective and cost-efficient for long video QA, enabling temporally grounded reasoning without dense processing.

title: Train, Retrieve, or Both? A Four-Arm Head-to-Head for Correct Statutory Citation on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act
summary: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need to be pointed at the provision of law that actually governs a question, with a correct statutory citation. We study this task on the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (RTA) and its core regulation, asking the operator's question empirically: is fine-tuning enough, or is hybrid retrieval needed? We run a four-arm head-to-head on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, and an SFT+RAG hybrid), scored on citation exact-match (section+subsection) over a small, human-verification-pending real eval set. The base model cannot cite the RTA and SFT-only mis-recalls sections; retrieval is essential and drives hallucination to zero by construction; and the SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. Its edge comes from SFT making provision selection more robust to the higher-recall candidate sets that hurt zero-shot RAG. Notably, this cheap bge-small hybrid matches or beats a pipeline built on bigger, specialized retrieval models (a larger embedder and a cross-encoder reranker), and a larger/improved training set does not help either: strong statutory-citation performance here does not require specialized retrieval models or more data. The artifact zeroes hallucination and clears the lift-over-base bar but does not reach the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target. All results are on a small, human-verification-pending real eval set and are reported as preliminary.

AI.tldr: This paper studies statutory citation retrieval for the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, comparing four approaches (base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only, SFT+RAG hybrid). The hybrid achieves the best exact-match score (0.481) with zero hallucinated citations, showing retrieval is essential and SFT improves robustness. Surprisingly, a cheap bge-small hybrid matches larger specialized retrieval models, and more data does not help. Results are preliminary on a small evaluation set.
AI.motivation: Self-represented tenants, landlords, and help-desk staff need accurate statutory citations pointing to the governing provision of law, but it is unclear whether fine-tuning alone suffices or hybrid retrieval is necessary for this task.
AI.method: Four-arm comparison on Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct: base zero-shot, LoRA SFT-only, RAG-only (bge-small retrieval), and SFT+RAG hybrid. Evaluated by citation exact-match (section+subsection) on a small human-verification-pending real eval set of the Ontario RTA.
AI.result: SFT+RAG hybrid scores highest at 0.481 exact-match with zero hallucinated citations. Base model cannot cite, SFT-only mis-recalls sections, RAG-only is weaker than hybrid. The cheap bge-small hybrid matches larger specialized retrieval pipelines (bigger embedder + cross-encoder reranker). More training data does not improve performance.
AI.conclusion: Retrieval is essential for zero-hallucination statutory citation; SFT adds robustness to higher-recall candidate sets. However, the best result (0.481) still falls short of the aspirational 0.70 exact-match target. Results are preliminary.

title: JanusMesh: Fast and Zero-Shot 3D Visual Illusion Generation via Cross-Space Denoising
summary: Creating 3D visual illusions, a single 3D mesh that reveals entirely different semantics from various viewing angles, is a fascinating but tough challenge. Existing optimization-based methods are slow and can produce oversaturated colors. In contrast, naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects. This results in visible unnatural seams and semantic leaks. In this paper, we present a fast and training-free framework for generating text-driven 3D visual illusions. Our approach decouples the generation into two stages. First, we propose a cross-space dual-branch denoising process. This process dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and Signed Distance Field (SDF) blending, which ensures seamless geometric fusion. Second, we introduce a view-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our method generates highly realistic, dual-semantic 3D illusions in just 3-5 minutes. It significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency. Project page: https://siang1105.github.io/JanusMesh.github.io/

AI.tldr: JanusMesh is a fast, training-free framework for generating text-driven 3D visual illusions (dual-semantic 3D meshes) in 3-5 minutes. It uses a cross-space dual-branch denoising process with CLIP-guided orientation alignment and SDF blending for seamless geometry, plus a view-conditioned texture synthesis module for realistic textures. It outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Creating 3D visual illusions (single mesh with different semantics from different viewing angles) is fascinating but challenging. Existing optimization-based methods are slow and produce oversaturated colors, while naive stitching approaches fail to produce geometrically coherent objects, resulting in unnatural seams and semantic leaks.
AI.method: A fast, training-free two-stage framework: (1) Cross-space dual-branch denoising process that dynamically decodes 3D latents into voxel space for CLIP-guided orientation alignment and SDF blending to ensure seamless geometric fusion; (2) View-conditioned texture synthesis module that projects and aggregates view-specific 2D diffusion priors onto the fused geometry.
AI.result: Generates highly realistic, dual-semantic 3D illusions in just 3-5 minutes. Significantly outperforms existing methods in geometric integrity, semantic recognizability, and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: JanusMesh achieves fast, high-quality 3D visual illusion generation without training, addressing key limitations of prior work in speed, color quality, geometric coherence, and semantic leakage.

title: Judging to Improve: A De-biased VLM-as-3D-Judge Protocol for Single-Image 3D Generation
summary: A companion study established a de-biased, cross-model VLM-as-3D-judge that reliably ranks single-image-to-3D mesh quality where cheap geometry and CLIP proxies fall short. This paper asks: can that judge's preferences specialize a strong open generator, TRELLIS, on one asset class (furniture), cheaply and without human labels? Taking the judge from ranking to optimization is where the work lives. Pushing a VLM judge into the training and evaluation loop exposes failure modes ranking never triggered, so our contribution is an optimization-grade hardening of the judge: a training judge (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) held distinct from an evaluation judge (InternVL3-8B) to break circularity; position-bias correction; and fixes for three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, and reference-free judging that rewards clean-but-wrong outputs), with calibration evidence (clear-gap win-rate 0.83-1.0; base-vs-base ~0.5). Using this protocol as an independent evaluator, and working only from public models and data with lightweight parameter-efficient adaptation, we find our methods match the strong base rather than exceed it. Independent base samples carry essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate), so signal must be engineered by quality-contrastive construction. Across six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep, the most targeted - conditioner repair under severe degradation - reaches parity (0.50) with the base, while no method clears the >=65% win-rate target. The result is mechanistic: clean inputs saturate the judge, flow-DIT fine-tuning washes out through the sampler, and conditioning repair is the locus that moves geometry. Win-rates are directional at n=8 objects. Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is itself informative: exceeding it needs more than lightweight PEFT on public data, and the judge protocol is reusable.

AI.tldr: A study attempting to specialize a strong open 3D generator (TRELLIS) to furniture using a VLM-as-judge for preference optimization, finding that lightweight adaptation matches the strong base but does not exceed it, with conditioning repair under severe degradation being the most effective method.
AI.motivation: To determine whether a VLM judge's preferences can cheaply specialize a strong open 3D generator (TRELLIS) to a specific asset class (furniture) without human labels, moving from ranking to optimization.
AI.method: Proposes an optimization-grade hardened VLM judge protocol with: separate training (Qwen2.5-VL-7B) and evaluation (InternVL3-8B) judges to break circularity; position-bias correction; fixes for three failure modes (image overload, geometry-hiding splat renders, reference-free judging). Tests six adaptation methods, two input regimes, and a severity sweep with parameter-efficient fine-tuning on public data.
AI.result: Methods match the strong base rather than exceed it; independent base samples show essentially no learnable preference (0.94 order-flip rate). Conditioner repair under severe degradation reaches parity (0.50) with the base; no method clears the ≥65% win-rate target. Win-rates are directional at n=8 objects.
AI.conclusion: Matching a strong public-data base with cheap adaptation is informative; exceeding it requires more than lightweight PEFT on public data. The judge protocol is reusable. Clean inputs saturate the judge, flow-DiT fine-tuning washes out through the sampler, and conditioning repair is the locus that moves geometry.

title: CRAX: Fast Safe Reinforcement Learning Benchmarking
summary: Safety is a core concern for deploying reinforcement learning (RL) agents in real-world domains such as robotics and autonomous driving. While benchmarks have been central to progress in RL, existing safety benchmarks with high-fidelity 3D physics remain computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping. To address this gap, we propose CRAX (Constrained RL Accelerated with JAX). Built on top of the MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics engine with realistic 3D dynamics, CRAX leverages vectorized operations and hardware acceleration, yielding up to ~100x speedups over comparable CPU-based safety benchmarks. The benchmark features six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks, each spanning three difficulty levels. Evaluating six popular safe RL methods shows that no single approach dominates across all tasks, and reveals the trade-offs between performance and safety. We find that curriculum learning across difficulty levels and safety transfer can improve performance over direct training in harder settings.

AI.tldr: CRAX is a fast, JAX-accelerated safe RL benchmark built on MJX physics, achieving ~100x speedup over CPU-based benchmarks, featuring 6 environment suites and 3 tasks across difficulty levels, revealing no single safe RL method dominates and curriculum learning improves safety transfer.
AI.motivation: Existing safety RL benchmarks with high-fidelity 3D physics are computationally slow, limiting large-scale experimentation and rapid prototyping.
AI.method: CRAX is built on the MuJoCo XLA (MJX) physics engine, leveraging vectorized operations and hardware acceleration to achieve up to ~100x speedups over comparable CPU-based safety benchmarks. It features six environment suites and three agent-specific tasks across three difficulty levels.
AI.result: Evaluating six popular safe RL methods shows no single approach dominates across all tasks, revealing trade-offs between performance and safety. Curriculum learning across difficulty levels and safety transfer can improve performance over direct training in harder settings.
AI.conclusion: CRAX provides a fast, scalable benchmark for safe RL research, demonstrating that curriculum learning and safety transfer are beneficial strategies, while highlighting the lack of a universally dominant safe RL method.

title: Towards Modality-imbalanced Federated Graph Learning: A Data Synthesis-based Approach
summary: MultiModal Federated Graph Learning (MM-FGL) offers a natural collaborative training paradigm, but its practical deployment is challenged by two granularities of modality imbalance. Client-level imbalance occurs when certain clients lack entire modalities, while node-level imbalance occurs when individual nodes exhibit missing visual or textual attributes. While several relevant studies exist, our investigation reveals that they predominantly target graph-agnostic or centralized scenarios, rendering them difficult to adapt directly. To address these challenges, we formalize modality-imbalanced MM-FGL as an implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem. This paradigm recovers missing modal semantics directly within the representation space, thereby maximizing alignment with the original data's semantic distribution and mitigating the high variance induced by missing modalities. To this end, we propose FedMGS (Federated Modality-aware Graph Synthesis), which integrates three core components. The availability-aware graph encoder prevents missing modalities from contaminating local structural propagation. The prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer establishes cross-client semantic anchors for unavailable modalities. The reliability-calibrated semantic fusion mechanism regulates the impact of recovered latent representations prior to predictive readout. Extensive experiments on four tasks show that FedMGS consistently outperforms competitive baselines with gains up to 17.41% with best efficiency-performance tradeoff.

AI.tldr: FedMGS addresses two granularities of modality imbalance (client-level and node-level) in MultiModal Federated Graph Learning by formulating it as a graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis problem, integrating availability-aware graph encoder, prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer, and reliability-calibrated semantic fusion to recover missing modal semantics, achieving up to 17.41% improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing MM-FGL methods fail to handle dual-granularity modality imbalance (missing entire modalities at client level or missing attributes at node level), and prior work focuses on graph-agnostic or centralized settings, making direct adaptation infeasible.
AI.method: Proposes FedMGS with three components: (1) availability-aware graph encoder to prevent missing modality contamination in local propagation, (2) prototype-guided latent semantic synthesizer for cross-client semantic anchoring of unavailable modalities, and (3) reliability-calibrated semantic fusion to regulate recovered representations before prediction.
AI.result: Outperforms baselines on four tasks with gains up to 17.41%, achieving the best efficiency-performance tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: Implicit graph-aware latent semantic representation synthesis is an effective paradigm for handling modality imbalance in MM-FGL, and FedMGS provides a practical solution with consistent gains.

title: The Significance of Style Diversity in Annotation-Free Synthetic Data Generation
summary: Generating high-utility synthetic data for intent classification typically requires human-annotated seed data, which is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings. In this paper, we propose a framework for synthetic dialogue generation that works entirely without human-annotated data, relying solely on intent definitions. Our proposed dialogue generation framework utilizes two different types of topic and style attributes to improve data diversity. Also, we propose two novel post-hoc stylization models called Univ and Exam to transform synthetic LLM-generated utterances into more varied, human-like linguistic styles. To enhance data quality, we utilize an LLM-as-a-judge filtering process. Experimental results on both industrial and public datasets demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves up to 93.3% of the performance obtained using human-annotated training data. Crucially, the findings reveal that style diversity is more critical than topic diversity for synthetic data utility, as it prevents models from learning spurious stylistic correlations. Furthermore, the study shows that incorporating style attributes during the generation process is more effective than post-hoc style adaptation.

AI.tldr: A framework for generating synthetic intent classification data entirely without human annotation, using only intent definitions, achieving up to 93.3% of human-annotated data performance, with findings that style diversity is more critical than topic diversity.
AI.motivation: Human-annotated seed data is often unavailable in fast-paced industrial settings, creating a need for high-utility synthetic data generation that does not rely on any human annotation.
AI.method: Proposes a dialogue generation framework using only intent definitions with two types of topic and style attributes for diversity, introduces two post-hoc stylization models (Univ and Exam) for human-like linguistic style transformation, and applies LLM-as-a-judge filtering for quality enhancement.
AI.result: Achieves up to 93.3% of human-annotated training data performance on industrial and public datasets. Style diversity is more critical than topic diversity. Incorporating style attributes during generation is more effective than post-hoc adaptation.
AI.conclusion: Synthetic data can effectively replace human-annotated data for intent classification, with style diversity being the key factor for utility, and in-generation style incorporation outperforming post-hoc stylization.

title: Direct Advantage Estimation for Scalable and Sample-efficient Deep Reinforcement Learning
summary: Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) has been shown to improve the sample efficiency of deep reinforcement learning algorithms. However, its reliance on full environment observability limits its applicability in realistic settings, and its requirement to model transition probabilities incurs substantial computational overhead for high-dimensional observations. In the present work, we address both limitations. First, we extend the theoretical framework of DAE to partially observable domains with minimal modifications. Second, we reduce its computational complexity by introducing discrete latent dynamics models that efficiently approximate transition probabilities. We evaluate our approach on the Arcade Learning Environment and find that DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency.

AI.tldr: This paper extends Direct Advantage Estimation (DAE) to partially observable domains and reduces its computational complexity using discrete latent dynamics models, achieving high sample efficiency on the Arcade Learning Environment.
AI.motivation: DAE improves sample efficiency but suffers from two limitations: (1) it requires full environment observability, limiting real-world applicability; (2) modeling transition probabilities is computationally expensive for high-dimensional observations.
AI.method: (1) Extends DAE's theoretical framework to partially observable Markov decision processes (POMDPs) with minimal changes. (2) Introduces discrete latent dynamics models to approximate transition probabilities efficiently, reducing computational complexity.
AI.result: DAE scales effectively with function approximator capacity while retaining high sample efficiency on the Arcade Learning Environment.
AI.conclusion: The proposed extensions make DAE applicable to more realistic, partially observable scenarios with reduced computational overhead, without sacrificing sample efficiency.

title: Pseudo-Feature Padding: A Lightweight Defense Against False Data Injection in Power Grids
summary: Deep Neural Networks DNNs have achieved remarkable accuracy in various tasks including their application in CyberPhysical Systems CPS for detecting False Data Injection Attacks FDIA during critical operations However the unique infrastructure of CPS makes DNNs vulnerable to exploitation by attackers aiming to evade detection Additionally the distinct nature of CPS presents challenges for conventional defense mechanisms against FDIA This paper proposes an innovative defense framework that strengthens DNNs against such attacks by introducing an additional input layer that performs padding in the input samples using pseudofeature values derived from the inputs statistical distribution This padding increases the input dimensionality in a randomized and dataaware manner making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible due to the nontransferable nature of crafted perturbations and the unpredictability of the padded structure Our method is lightweight modelagnostic and requires no modifications to the core architecture making it highly deployable in realworld CPS settings We evaluated our framework on critical power grid applications such as state estimation using the IEEE 14bus 30bus 118bus and 300bus systems Experiments under adversarial settings demonstrate that our padding strategy significantly improves model robustness with negligible impact on performance and effectively mitigates attacks that would otherwise bypass conventional defenses

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a lightweight, model-agnostic defense framework for DNNs in CPS that adds a padding layer using pseudofeatures from the input's statistical distribution. This increases input dimensionality to make adversarial attacks computationally infeasible, evaluated on power grid state estimation (IEEE 14, 30, 118, 300-bus systems), showing improved robustness with negligible performance loss.
AI.motivation: DNNs achieve high accuracy in CPS for detecting False Data Injection Attacks (FDIA), but CPS infrastructure makes DNNs vulnerable to adversarial exploitation, and conventional defense mechanisms face challenges against FDIA in these unique settings.
AI.method: Introduces an additional input layer that pads input samples with pseudofeature values derived from the input's statistical distribution. This padding is randomized and data-aware, increasing input dimensionality in a way that makes crafted adversarial perturbations nontransferable and unpredictable.
AI.result: Evaluated on IEEE 14, 30, 118, and 300-bus power grid systems. Under adversarial settings, the padding strategy significantly improves model robustness with negligible impact on performance, effectively mitigating attacks that bypass conventional defenses.
AI.conclusion: The proposed padding defense is lightweight, model-agnostic, requires no core architecture modifications, and is highly deployable in real-world CPS, effectively making adversarial attacks computationally infeasible.

title: On the Redundancy of Timestep Embeddings in Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models rely heavily on explicit timestep embeddings to modulate the denoising process across various noise scales. In this work, we challenge the necessity of these temporal signals by analyzing their impact on U-Net and Diffusion Transformer architectures. Beyond empirical evidence, we provide a theoretical framework demonstrating that, under certain conditions, the global minimizer of the diffusion training objective can be achieved without explicit timestep conditioning. Our findings reveal a surprising robustness when timestep embeddings are completely removed. Extensive ablation studies on the CelebA and CIFAR-10 datasets show that these time-agnostic models can maintain high structural fidelity and even surpass their conditioned counterparts in competitive metrics, including FID, precision, and recall. Our analysis suggests these architectures can implicitly infer noise scales from the corrupted input under specific assumptions, rendering explicit temporal conditioning redundant. This study challenges long-standing temporal conditioning paradigms and paves the way for more efficient and structurally focused generative architectures.
